title: Sacra Scriptura — Vulgata Clementina
description: Scripture readings from the Clementine Vulgate for First Form Latin (Gospel of John) and Second Form Latin (First, Second, and Third Epistles of John). Each passage includes copywork, English derivatives, and grammar exercises (parsing and identification).

passages.title: Iōhannes 1:1–3 · In Prīncipiō
passages.latin: In principio erat Verbum, et Verbum erat apud Deum, et Deus erat Verbum. Hoc erat in principio apud Deum. Omnia per ipsum facta sunt, et sine ipso factum est nihil quod factum est.
passages.english: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. The same was in the beginning with God. All things were made by him: and without him was made nothing that was made.
passages.notes: The opening of John's Gospel. Verbum (Word) is neuter, 2nd declension. Deus is masculine, 2nd declension. In principio: ablative of time. The verb erat (was) is imperfect of sum.

sentences: In principio erat Verbum.
Deus erat Verbum.
Omnia per ipsum facta sunt.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence carefully. Circle every nominative noun. Underline every verb. Draw a box around any preposition and its object.

parsing.answer: principium, -ii, n. — ablative singular; object of preposition in
verbum, -i, n. — nominative singular; predicate nominative after erat
sum, esse — imperfect active indicative, 3rd person singular; 'was'
Deus, -i, m. — accusative singular; object of preposition apud

identification.instruction: Find all nominative nouns in John 1:1–3. List each with its gender and why it is nominative.
identification.answer: Verbum (neuter, subject or predicate nom. of erat — 3×); Deus (masculine, predicate nominative of erat — 2×); nihil (neuter indeclinable, subject of factum est)

passages.title: Iōhannes 1:4–9 · Lūx Vēra
passages.latin: In ipso vita erat, et vita erat lux hominum; et lux in tenebris lucet, et tenebrae eam non comprehenderunt. Fuit homo missus a Deo, cui nomen erat Ioannes. Hic venit in testimonium ut testimonium perhiberet de lumine, ut omnes crederent per illum. Non erat ille lux, sed ut testimonium perhiberet de lumine. Erat lux vera quae illuminat omnem hominem venientem in hunc mundum.
passages.english: In him was life, and the life was the light of men. And the light shineth in darkness, and the darkness did not comprehend it. There was a man sent from God, whose name was John. This man came for a witness, to give testimony of the light, that all men might believe through him. He was not the light, but was to give testimony of the light. That was the true light, which enlighteneth every man that cometh into this world.
passages.notes: Key vocabulary: vita (life), lux (light) — 3rd declension. Tenebrae, -arum (darkness) — 1st declension plural. Note all five cases of 1st declension nouns in this passage.

sentences: In ipso vita erat, et vita erat lux hominum.
Lux in tenebris lucet.
Erat lux vera.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. For each noun, write its case above it (Nom., Gen., Dat., Acc., Abl.). Circle all 1st-declension nouns.

parsing.answer: vita, -ae, f. — nominative singular; subject of erat
homo, hominis, m. — genitive plural; 'of men' (possession)
tenebrae, -arum, f. — ablative plural; object of preposition in
lumen, luminis, n. — ablative singular; object of preposition de

identification.instruction: List every 1st-declension noun in John 1:4–9. Give its case and number for each occurrence.
identification.answer: vita (nom. sg. — 2×); vita (nom. sg. — predicate); tenebris (abl. pl.); Ioannes (nom. sg. — proper name, 1st decl.)

passages.title: Iōhannes 1:10–14 · Verbum Carō Factum Est
passages.latin: In mundo erat, et mundus per ipsum factus est, et mundus eum non cognovit. In propria venit, et sui eum non receperunt. Quotquot autem receperunt eum, dedit eis potestatem filios Dei fieri, his qui credunt in nomine eius. Qui non ex sanguinibus, neque ex voluntate carnis, neque ex voluntate viri, sed ex Deo nati sunt. Et Verbum caro factum est et habitavit in nobis; et vidimus gloriam eius, gloriam quasi unigeniti a Patre, plenum gratiae et veritatis.
passages.english: He was in the world, and the world was made by him, and the world knew him not. He came unto his own, and his own received him not. But as many as received him, he gave them power to be made the sons of God, to them that believe in his name. Who are born, not of blood, nor of the will of the flesh, nor of the will of man, but of God. And the Word was made flesh and dwelt among us; and we saw his glory, the glory as it were of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.
passages.notes: The Incarnation: Et Verbum caro factum est. Review passage covering 1st declension and 1st conjugation present active. Mundo, -i (world) is 2nd declension. Caro, carnis (flesh) is 3rd declension.

sentences: Et Verbum caro factum est.
Vidimus gloriam eius.
Mundus per ipsum factus est.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify the tense of every verb (present, imperfect, or perfect). Circle nouns in the 1st declension. Underline verbs in the 1st conjugation.

parsing.prompt: Parse: factum est (in 'Verbum caro factum est')
parsing.answer: facio, facere — perfect passive indicative, 3rd person singular; 'was made'
recipio, recipere — perfect active indicative, 3rd person plural; 'received'
credo, credere — present active indicative, 3rd person plural; 'believe'
habito, habitare — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular; 'dwelt, lived'

identification.instruction: Find all 1st conjugation verbs in John 1:10–14. List them in their present active infinitive form.
identification.answer: habitare (habitavit); credere is 3rd conj.; recipere is 3rd conj. — 1st conj. present actives: habitat (implied from habitavit). Also note: cognovit (cognosco, 3rd), receperunt (recipio, 3rd-io), credunt (credo, 3rd)

passages.title: Iōhannes 1:29–34 · Ecce Agnus Deī
passages.latin: Altera die vidit Ioannes Iesum venientem ad se et ait: Ecce agnus Dei, ecce qui tollit peccatum mundi. Hic est de quo dixi: Post me venit vir qui ante me factus est, quia prior me erat. Et ego nesciebam eum, sed ut manifestaretur in Israel, propterea veni ego in aqua baptizans. Et testimonium perhibuit Ioannes dicens: Quia vidi Spiritum descendentem quasi columbam de caelo, et mansit super eum. Et ego nesciebam eum; sed qui misit me baptizare in aqua, ille mihi dixit: Super quem videris Spiritum descendentem et manentem super eum, hic est qui baptizat in Spiritu Sancto. Et ego vidi, et testimonium perhibui quia hic est Filius Dei.
passages.english: The next day, John saw Jesus coming to him, and he saith: Behold the Lamb of God, behold him who taketh away the sin of the world. This is he of whom I said: After me there cometh a man, who is preferred before me: because he was before me. And I knew him not, but that he may be made manifest in Israel, therefore am I come baptizing with water. And John gave testimony, saying: I saw the Spirit coming down, as a dove from heaven, and he remained upon him. And I knew him not; but he who sent me to baptize with water, said to me: He upon whom thou shalt see the Spirit descending, and remaining upon him, he it is that baptizeth with the Holy Ghost. And I saw, and I gave testimony, that this is the Son of God.
passages.notes: John the Baptist's testimony. Agnus, -i (lamb) — 2nd declension masculine. Filius, -ii (son) — 2nd declension masculine. Spiritus (spirit) — 4th declension (preview). Focus: 2nd declension masculine nouns.

sentences: Ecce agnus Dei.
Hic est Filius Dei.
Vidi Spiritum descendentem de caelo.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Circle every 2nd-declension masculine noun. Write its case beneath it. Underline the main verb.

parsing.answer: agnus, -i, m. — nominative singular; subject (predicate after ecce)
Deus, -i, m. — genitive singular; 'of God' (possessive genitive)
filius, -ii, m. — nominative singular; predicate nominative after est
vir, viri, m. — nominative singular; subject of venit

identification.instruction: Find all 2nd-declension masculine nouns in John 1:29–34 and list each with its case.
identification.answer: agnus (nom. sg.); Dei (gen. sg.); peccatum (nom./acc. sg. — 2nd decl. neuter); mundi (gen. sg.); vir (nom. sg.); filius (nom. sg.); Spiritum (acc. sg. — 4th decl., preview); caelo (abl. sg.)

passages.title: Iōhannes 2:1–11 · Nuptiae in Cānā
passages.latin: Et die tertia nuptiae factae sunt in Cana Galilaeae, et erat mater Iesu ibi. Vocatus est autem et Iesus, et discipuli eius, ad nuptias. Et deficiente vino, dicit mater Iesu ad eum: Vinum non habent. Et dicit ei Iesus: Quid mihi et tibi est, mulier? nondum venit hora mea. Dicit mater eius ministris: Quodcumque dixerit vobis, facite. Erant autem ibi lapideae hydriae sex positae secundum purificationem Iudaeorum, capientes singulae metretas binas vel ternas. Dicit eis Iesus: Implete hydrias aqua. Et impleverunt eas usque ad summum. Et dicit eis Iesus: Haurire nunc, et ferte architriclino. Et tulerunt. Ut autem gustavit architriclinus aquam vinum factam... hoc fecit initium signorum Iesus.
passages.english: And the third day, there was a marriage in Cana of Galilee: and the mother of Jesus was there. And Jesus also was invited, and his disciples, to the marriage. And the wine failing, the mother of Jesus saith to him: They have no wine. And Jesus saith to her: Woman, what is that to me and to thee? my hour is not yet come. His mother saith to the waiters: Whatsoever he shall say to you, do ye. Now there were set there six waterpots of stone, according to the manner of the purifying of the Jews, containing two or three measures apiece. Jesus saith to them: Fill the waterpots with water. And they filled them up to the brim. And Jesus saith to them: Draw out now and carry to the chief steward of the feast... This beginning of miracles did Jesus.
passages.notes: The Wedding at Cana. Focus: 2nd declension plural forms and neuter nouns. Vinum, -i (wine) is neuter 2nd declension. Signum, -i (sign/miracle) is neuter 2nd declension. Neuter rule: nominative = accusative.

sentences: Vinum non habent.
Quodcumque dixerit vobis, facite.
Hoc fecit initium signorum Iesus.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Box every neuter noun. Label it N (nominative) or A (accusative). Remember: neuter nominative = neuter accusative.

parsing.answer: vinum, -i, n. — accusative singular; direct object of habent (or nominative — same form for neuter)
nuptiae, -arum, f. pl. — nominative plural; subject of factae sunt
signum, -i, n. — genitive plural; 'of signs/miracles'
initium, -ii, n. — accusative singular; direct object of fecit

identification.instruction: Find all neuter nouns in John 2:1–11. For each, state whether it is nominative or accusative (or both, if ambiguous).
identification.answer: vinum (acc. sg.); signum / signorum (gen. pl. — genitive, not ambiguous); initium (acc. sg.); Cana (proper noun, indeclinable)

passages.title: Iōhannes 3:1–8 · Nisi Quis Renātus Fuerit
passages.latin: Erat autem homo ex Pharisaeis, Nicodemus nomine, princeps Iudaeorum. Hic venit ad Iesum nocte et dixit ei: Rabbi, scimus quia a Deo venisti magister; nemo enim potest haec signa facere quae tu facis, nisi fuerit Deus cum eo. Respondit Iesus et dixit ei: Amen, amen dico tibi, nisi quis natus fuerit denuo, non potest videre regnum Dei. Dicit ad eum Nicodemus: Quomodo potest homo nasci cum senex sit? Respondit Iesus: Amen, amen dico tibi, nisi quis renatus fuerit ex aqua et Spiritu Sancto, non potest introire in regnum Dei. Quod natum est ex carne, caro est; et quod natum est ex spiritu, spiritus est. Non mireris quia dixi tibi: Oportet vos nasci denuo. Spiritus ubi vult spirat.
passages.english: And there was a man of the Pharisees, named Nicodemus, a ruler of the Jews. This man came to Jesus by night, and said to him: Rabbi, we know that thou art come a teacher from God; for no man can do these signs which thou dost, unless God be with him. Jesus answered, and said to him: Amen, amen I say to thee, unless a man be born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God. Nicodemus saith to him: How can a man be born when he is old? Jesus answered: Amen, amen I say to thee, unless a man be born again of water and the Holy Ghost, he cannot enter into the kingdom of God. That which is born of the flesh, is flesh; and that which is born of the Spirit, is spirit. Wonder not, that I said to thee, you must be born again. The Spirit breatheth where he will.
passages.notes: Nicodemus and the new birth. Focus: imperfect tense (-ba- sign). Erat (was), poterat (was able), dixit (said — perfect). Watch for imperfect forms throughout.

sentences: Erat autem homo ex Pharisaeis.
Nisi quis renatus fuerit ex aqua et Spiritu Sancto, non potest introire in regnum Dei.
Spiritus ubi vult spirat.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Underline every imperfect tense verb (hint: look for -ba-). Circle every perfect tense verb.

parsing.answer: sum, esse — imperfect active indicative, 3rd person singular; 'was'
possum, posse — present active indicative, 3rd person singular; 'is able, can'

parsing.prompt: Parse: venit (in 'Hic venit ad Iesum nocte')
parsing.answer: venio, venire — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular; 'came'
spiro, spirare — present active indicative, 3rd person singular; 'breathes, blows'

identification.instruction: Find all imperfect tense verbs in John 3:1–8. Write each verb and its subject.
identification.answer: erat (homo — 'there was a man'); The passage uses primarily perfect tense verbs (venit, dixit, respondit) and present tense; erat is the main imperfect. Students should recognize erat as imperfect of sum.

passages.title: Iōhannes 3:14–21 · Sīc Deus Dīlēxit Mundum
passages.latin: Et sicut Moyses exaltavit serpentem in deserto, ita exaltari oportet Filium hominis, ut omnis qui credit in ipsum non pereat, sed habeat vitam aeternam. Sic enim Deus dilexit mundum, ut Filium suum unigenitum daret, ut omnis qui credit in eum non pereat, sed habeat vitam aeternam. Non enim misit Deus Filium suum in mundum ut iudicet mundum, sed ut salvetur mundus per ipsum. Qui credit in eum non iudicatur; qui autem non credit, iam iudicatus est, quia non credidit in nomine unigeniti Filii Dei. Hoc est autem iudicium: quia lux venit in mundum, et dilexerunt homines magis tenebras quam lucem: erant enim eorum mala opera. Omnis enim qui male agit, odit lucem.
passages.english: And as Moses lifted up the serpent in the desert, so must the Son of man be lifted up: That whosoever believeth in him, may not perish; but may have life everlasting. For God so loved the world, as to give his only begotten Son; that whosoever believeth in him, may not perish, but may have life everlasting. For God sent not his Son into the world, to judge the world, but that the world may be saved by him. He that believeth in him is not judged. But he that doth not believe, is already judged: because he believeth not in the name of the only begotten Son of God. And this is the judgment: because the light is come into the world, and men loved darkness rather than the light: for their works were evil. For every one that doth evil, hateth the light.
passages.notes: John 3:16 — perhaps the most memorized verse in Christendom. Review passage: 2nd declension, imperfect tense, future tense. Dilexit (loved) is perfect of diligo. Aeternam (eternal) is a 1st/2nd declension adjective.

sentences: Sic enim Deus dilexit mundum.
Omnis qui credit in eum non pereat, sed habeat vitam aeternam.
Lux venit in mundum.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Label every verb with its tense (Pres., Imperf., Perf., Fut.). Underline every 2nd-declension noun.

parsing.answer: diligo, diligere — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular; 'loved'
do, dare — imperfect active subjunctive, 3rd person singular; 'might give' (purpose clause)
credo, credere — present active indicative, 3rd person singular; 'believes'
salvo, salvare — present passive subjunctive, 3rd person singular; 'may be saved' (purpose clause)

identification.instruction: John 3:16 contains three verbs. Identify all three with tense, voice, and what they tell us about God's action.
identification.answer: dilexit — perfect active (God loved, completed action); daret — imperfect subjunctive (that he might give, purpose); pereat/habeat — present subjunctive (ongoing result: may not perish, but may have)

passages.title: Iōhannes 4:4–15 · Aqua Vīva
passages.latin: Oportebat autem eum transire per Samariam. Venit ergo in civitatem Samariae quae dicitur Sichar. Erat autem ibi fons Jacob. Iesus ergo fatigatus ex itinere sedebat sic supra fontem. Hora erat quasi sexta. Venit mulier de Samaria haurire aquam. Dicit ei Iesus: Da mihi bibere. Si scires donum Dei et quis est qui dicit tibi: Da mihi bibere; tu forsitan petisses ab eo, et dedisset tibi aquam vivam. Dicit ei mulier: Domine, neque in quo haurias habes, et puteus altus est; unde ergo habes aquam vivam? Respondit Iesus et dixit ei: Omnis qui bibit ex aqua hac, sitiet iterum; qui autem biberit ex aqua quam ego dabo ei, non sitiet in aeternum. Sed aqua quam dabo ei, fiet in eo fons aquae salientis in vitam aeternam.
passages.english: And he was of necessity to pass through Samaria. He cometh therefore to a city of Samaria, which is called Sichar. Now Jacob's well was there. Jesus therefore being wearied with his journey, sat thus on the well. It was about the sixth hour. There cometh a woman of Samaria to draw water. Jesus saith to her: Give me to drink. If thou didst know the gift of God and who he is that saith to thee: Give me to drink; thou perhaps wouldst have asked of him, and he would have given thee living water. The woman saith to him: Sir, thou hast nothing wherein to draw, and the well is deep; from whence then hast thou living water? Jesus answered, and said to her: Whosoever drinketh of this water, shall thirst again; but he that shall drink of the water that I will give him, shall not thirst for ever. But the water that I will give him, shall become in him a fountain of water, springing up into life everlasting.
passages.notes: The Woman at the Well. Focus: 3rd declension nouns. Fons, fontis (spring/well) — 3rd decl. m. Mulier, mulieris (woman) — 3rd decl. f. Aqua viva (living water) — note 1st decl. aqua with adjective.

sentences: Erat autem ibi fons Jacob.
Omnis qui bibit ex aqua hac, sitiet iterum.
Aqua quam dabo ei fiet in eo fons aquae salientis in vitam aeternam.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Circle every 3rd-declension noun. Write its case above it. Find the stem of each 3rd-declension noun from its genitive.

parsing.answer: fons, fontis, m. — nominative singular; subject of erat
mulier, mulieris, f. — nominative singular; subject of venit
aqua, -ae, f. — accusative singular; direct object of haurire
fons, fontis, m. — accusative singular; object of preposition supra

identification.instruction: List all 3rd-declension nouns in John 4:4–15. For each, give the nominative, genitive, gender, and meaning.
identification.answer: fons, fontis, m. (spring/well); mulier, mulieris, f. (woman); iter, itineris, n. (journey — in itinere); civitas, civitatis, f. (city — in civitatem); hora, -ae is 1st decl., not 3rd

passages.title: Iōhannes 4:46–54 · Fīlius Rēgulī
passages.latin: Venit ergo iterum in Cana Galilaeae, ubi fecit aquam vinum. Et erat quidam regulus cuius filius infirmabatur Capharnaum. Hic cum audisset quia Iesus adveniret in Galilaeam a Iudaea, abiit ad eum et rogabat eum ut descenderet et sanaret filium eius; incipiebat enim mori. Dixit ergo Iesus ad eum: Nisi signa et prodigia videritis, non creditis. Dicit ad eum regulus: Domine, descende priusquam moriatur filius meus. Dicit ei Iesus: Vade, filius tuus vivit. Credidit homo sermoni quem dixit ei Iesus, et ibat. Iam autem eo descendente, servi occurrerunt ei et nuntiaverunt dicentes quia filius eius viveret. Interrogavit ergo horam ab eis in qua melius habuerit. Et dixerunt ei: Quia heri hora septima reliquit eum febris. Cognovit ergo pater quia illa hora erat in qua dixit ei Iesus: Filius tuus vivit.
passages.english: He came again therefore into Cana of Galilee, where he made the water wine. And there was a certain ruler, whose son was sick at Capernaum. He, when he had heard that Jesus was come from Judea into Galilee, went to him and desired him to come down and heal his son; for he was at the point of death. Jesus therefore said to him: Unless you see signs and wonders, you believe not. The ruler saith to him: Lord, come down before my son die. Jesus saith to him: Go thy way; thy son liveth. The man believed the word which Jesus said to him, and went his way. And as he was going down, his servants met him; and they brought word saying, that his son lived. He asked therefore the hour of them at which he recovered. And they said to him: Yesterday, at the seventh hour, the fever left him. The father therefore knew, that it was at the same hour that Jesus said to him, Thy son liveth.
passages.notes: The Royal Official's son healed. Focus: 3rd declension plural; neuter 3rd declension. Sermo, sermonis (word/speech) — 3rd decl. m. Hora, -ae — 1st decl. Prodigium, -ii — 2nd decl. neuter.

sentences: Filius tuus vivit.
Credidit homo sermoni quem dixit ei Iesus.
Heri hora septima reliquit eum febris.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify each noun as 1st, 2nd, or 3rd declension. For 3rd declension nouns, write the genitive form.

parsing.answer: sermo, sermonis, m. — dative singular; indirect object of credidit ('believed in/to the word')
servus, -i, m. — nominative plural; subject of occurrerunt
signum, -i, n. — accusative plural; direct object of videritis
prodigium, -ii, n. — accusative plural; direct object of videritis; neuter 2nd declension

identification.instruction: Find all plural nouns in John 4:46–54. Identify each as 1st, 2nd, or 3rd declension and state its case.
identification.answer: signa (2nd decl. neuter, acc. pl.); prodigia (2nd decl. neuter, acc. pl.); servi (2nd decl. masc., nom. pl.); servi (2nd decl., gen. sg. — check context)

passages.title: Iōhannes 5:1–9 · Surge, Tolle Grabātum Tuum
passages.latin: Post haec erat dies festus Iudaeorum, et ascendit Iesus Hierosolymam. Est autem Hierosolymis probatica piscina, quae cognominatur Hebraice Bethsaida, quinque porticus habens. In his iacebat multitudo magna languentium, caecorum, claudorum, aridorum, exspectantium aquae motum. Angelus autem Domini descendebat secundum tempus in piscinam et movebatur aqua. Et qui prior descendisset post motionem aquae, sanus fiebat a quacumque detinebatur infirmitate. Erat autem ibi homo triginta et octo annos habens in infirmitate sua. Hunc cum vidisset Iesus iacentem, et cognovisset quia multum iam tempus habet, dicit ei: Vis sanus fieri? Respondit ei languidus: Domine, hominem non habeo, ut, cum turbata fuerit aqua, mittat me in piscinam. Dicit ei Iesus: Surge, tolle grabatum tuum et ambula. Et statim sanus factus est homo ille, et sustulit grabatum suum et ambulabat.
passages.english: After these things was a festival day of the Jews, and Jesus went up to Jerusalem. Now there is at Jerusalem a pond, called Probatica, which in Hebrew is named Bethsaida, having five porches. In these lay a great multitude of sick, of blind, of lame, of withered; waiting for the moving of the water. And an angel of the Lord descended at certain times into the pond; and the water was moved. And he that went down first after the motion of the water, was made whole, of whatsoever infirmity he lay under. And there was a certain man there, that had been eight and thirty years under his infirmity. Him when Jesus had seen lying, and knew that he had been now a long time, he saith to him: Wilt thou be made whole? The infirm man answered him: Sir, I have no man, when the water is troubled, to put me into the pond. Jesus saith to him: Arise, take up thy bed, and walk. And immediately the man was made whole: and he took up his bed, and walked.
passages.notes: The Healing at Bethesda. Focus: 2nd conjugation verbs (all three tenses — present, imperfect, future). Viderit (saw) — 3rd conj. Habeo (have) — 2nd conj. Iacebat (was lying) — 2nd conj. imperfect.

sentences: Surge, tolle grabatum tuum et ambula.
Et statim sanus factus est homo ille.
Angelus descendebat in piscinam et movebatur aqua.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Underline every 2nd-conjugation verb. Write P (present), I (imperfect), or F (future) above each verb.

parsing.answer: iaceo, iacere — imperfect active indicative, 3rd person singular; 2nd conjugation; 'was lying'
video, videre — pluperfect active subjunctive, 3rd person singular; 2nd conjugation; 'had seen'
descendo, descendere — imperfect active indicative, 3rd person singular; 3rd conjugation; 'was descending'
habeo, habere — present active participle, nominative singular; 2nd conjugation; 'having'

identification.instruction: Find all 2nd-conjugation verbs in John 5:1–9. List each in present active infinitive form.
identification.answer: iacere (iacebat, iacentem); videre (vidisset); habere (habens, habet); movere (movebatur — passive); fieri (fiebat — irregular but from facio)

passages.title: Iōhannes 6:1–15 · Pānēs et Piscēs
passages.latin: Post haec abiit Iesus trans mare Galilaeae, quod est Tiberiadis. Et sequebatur eum multitudo magna, quia videbant signa quae faciebat super his qui infirmabantur. Subiit ergo in montem Iesus, et ibi sedebat cum discipulis suis. Erat autem proximum Pascha dies festus Iudaeorum. Cum sublevasset ergo oculos Iesus et vidisset quia multitudo magna venit ad eum, dicit ad Philippum: Unde ememus panes, ut manducent hi? Respondit ei unus ex discipulis eius, Andreas: Est puer unus hic qui habet quinque panes hordeaceos et duos pisces; sed haec quid sunt inter tantos? Dixit ergo Iesus: Facite homines discumbere. Erat autem foenum multum in loco. Discubuerunt ergo viri, numero quasi quinque milia. Accepit ergo Iesus panes et cum gratias egisset, distribuit discumbentibus; similiter et ex piscibus quantum volebant. Ut autem impleti sunt, dixit discipulis suis: Colligite quae superaverunt fragmenta, ne pereant. Collegerunt ergo et impleverunt duodecim cophinos fragmentorum ex quinque panibus hordeaceis quae superfuerunt his qui manducaverant.
passages.english: After these things Jesus went over the sea of Galilee, which is that of Tiberias. And a great multitude followed him, because they saw the miracles which he did on them that were diseased. Jesus therefore went up into a mountain, and there he sat with his disciples. Now the pasch, the festival day of the Jews, was near at hand. When Jesus therefore had lifted up his eyes and seen that a very great multitude cometh to him, he said to Philip: Whence shall we buy bread, that these may eat? One of his disciples, Andrew, the brother of Simon Peter, saith to him: There is a boy here that hath five barley loaves and two fishes; but what are these among so many? Then Jesus said: Make the men sit down. Now there was much grass in the place. The men therefore sat down, in number about five thousand. And Jesus took the loaves; and when he had given thanks, he distributed to them that were set down. In like manner also of the fishes, as much as they would. And when they were filled, he said to his disciples: Gather up the fragments that remain, lest they be lost. They gathered therefore, and filled twelve baskets with the fragments of the five barley loaves, which remained over and above to them that had eaten.
passages.notes: The Feeding of the Five Thousand. Review: 3rd declension, 2nd conjugation, sum. Panes (pl. of panis, 3rd decl.) and pisces (pl. of piscis, 3rd decl.) — note plural forms. Gratias egisset — to give thanks.

sentences: Est puer unus hic qui habet quinque panes et duos pisces.
Colligite quae superaverunt fragmenta, ne pereant.
Accepit Iesus panes et cum gratias egisset distribuit discumbentibus.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Circle every 3rd-declension noun. Underline every 2nd-conjugation verb. Put brackets around any form of sum (esse).

parsing.answer: panis, panis, m. — accusative plural; 3rd declension; direct object of habet
piscis, piscis, m. — accusative plural; 3rd declension; direct object of habet
discumbo, discumbere — present active participle, dative plural; 'to those reclining'
sum, esse — imperfect active indicative, 3rd person singular; 'was'

identification.instruction: List every 3rd-declension noun in John 6:1–15 (at least 5). Give nominative, genitive, gender, and case as used.
identification.answer: panis, panis, m. (panes — acc. pl.); piscis, piscis, m. (pisces — acc. pl.); multitudo, multitudinis, f. (nom. sg.); mons, montis, m. (montem — acc. sg.); fragmentum is 2nd decl. neuter

passages.title: Iōhannes 6:35–40 · Ego Sum Pānis Vītae
passages.latin: Dixit autem eis Iesus: Ego sum panis vitae; qui venit ad me, non esuriet, et qui credit in me, non sitiet umquam. Sed dixi vobis quia et vidistis me, et non credidistis. Omne quod dat mihi Pater, ad me veniet; et eum qui venit ad me, non eiciam foras. Quia descendi de caelo, non ut faciam voluntatem meam, sed voluntatem eius qui misit me. Haec est autem voluntas eius qui misit me, Patris: ut omne quod dedit mihi, non perdam ex eo, sed resuscitem illud in novissimo die. Haec est autem voluntas Patris mei qui misit me: ut omnis qui videt Filium et credit in eum, habeat vitam aeternam, et ego resuscitabo eum in novissimo die.
passages.english: And Jesus said to them: I am the bread of life: he that cometh to me shall not hunger: and he that believeth in me shall never thirst. But I said unto you, that you also have seen me, and you believe not. All that the Father giveth to me shall come to me; and him that cometh to me, I will not cast out. Because I came down from heaven, not to do my own will, but the will of him that sent me. Now this is the will of the Father who sent me: that of all that he hath given me, I should lose nothing; but should raise it up again in the last day. And this is the will of my Father that sent me: that every one who seeth the Son, and believeth in him, may have life everlasting, and I will raise him up in the last day.
passages.notes: First 'I AM' saying of John's Gospel. Focus: adjective agreement — gender, number, case. Note voluntatem meam (fem. acc. sg.) — voluntas, -atis (3rd decl. f.) with meus, -a, -um. Novissimo die — superlative adjective.

sentences: Ego sum panis vitae.
Haec est voluntas Patris mei: ut omnis qui videt Filium habeat vitam aeternam.
Ego resuscitabo eum in novissimo die.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. For every adjective, draw an arrow to the noun it modifies. Write the gender (M/F/N), number (S/P), and case of the adjective above it.

parsing.answer: vita, -ae, f. — genitive singular; 'of life' (genitive of description)
aeternus, -a, -um — feminine accusative singular; agrees with vitam (vita, -ae, f.)
novissimus, -a, -um — masculine/neuter ablative singular; superlative of novus; agrees with die (dies, 5th decl. m.)
meus, -a, -um — feminine accusative singular; agrees with voluntatem (voluntas, -atis, f.)

identification.instruction: Find five adjective-noun pairs in John 6:35–40. For each pair, confirm that the adjective and noun agree in gender, number, and case.
identification.answer: vitam aeternam (fem. acc. sg.); voluntatem meam (fem. acc. sg.); novissimo die (masc. abl. sg.); vitam aeternam again; Pater meus (nom. sg. masc.)

passages.title: Iōhannes 8:12–18 · Ego Sum Lūx Mundī
passages.latin: Iterum ergo locutus est eis Iesus dicens: Ego sum lux mundi; qui sequitur me, non ambulat in tenebris, sed habebit lumen vitae. Dixerunt ergo ei Pharisaei: Tu de teipso testimonium perhibes; testimonium tuum non est verum. Respondit Iesus et dixit eis: Et si ego testimonium perhibeo de meipso, verum est testimonium meum, quia scio unde veni et quo vado; vos autem nescitis unde veniam, aut quo vadam. Vos secundum carnem iudicatis; ego non iudico quemquam. Et si iudico ego, iudicium meum verum est, quia solus non sum, sed ego et qui misit me Pater. Et in lege vestra scriptum est, quia duorum hominum testimonium verum est. Ego sum qui testimonium perhibeo de meipso, et testimonium perhibet de me qui misit me Pater.
passages.english: Again therefore, Jesus spoke to them, saying: I am the light of the world: he that followeth me, walketh not in darkness, but shall have the light of life. The Pharisees therefore said to him: Thou givest testimony of thyself; thy testimony is not true. Jesus answered, and said to them: Although I give testimony of myself, my testimony is true: for I know whence I came, and whither I go; but you know not whence I come, or whither I go. You judge according to the flesh; I judge not any man. And if I do judge, my judgment is true: because I am not alone, but I and the Father that sent me. And in your law it is written, that the testimony of two men is true. I am one that give testimony of myself, and the Father that sent me giveth testimony of me.
passages.notes: Second 'I AM' saying. Focus: personal pronouns. Ego (I), tu (you), vos (you pl.), nos — all appear here. Note how Latin can drop the pronoun when unambiguous, but uses it for emphasis: Ego sum lux mundi.

sentences: Ego sum lux mundi.
Qui sequitur me non ambulat in tenebris sed habebit lumen vitae.
Vos secundum carnem iudicatis; ego non iudico quemquam.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Circle every personal pronoun (ego, tu, vos, me, te, vobis, etc.). Write the person and number above each (1S, 2S, 1P, 2P, 3P, etc.).

parsing.answer: ego — 1st person personal pronoun, nominative singular; used for emphasis ('I myself')
ego — 1st person personal pronoun, accusative singular; direct object of sequitur
vos — 2nd person personal pronoun, nominative plural; subject of iudicatis (emphatic contrast with ego)
meus, -a, -um — neuter nominative singular possessive adjective; agrees with iudicium (n.)

identification.instruction: Find all personal pronouns in John 8:12–18. List each form, identify it (person, number, case), and note whether it is used for emphasis.
identification.answer: ego (1st sg. nom. — emphatic, 4×); me (1st sg. acc., 3×); meipso (1st sg. abl. reflex.); tu (2nd sg. nom.); tuum (2nd sg. gen. as possessive); vos (2nd pl. nom.); vestra (2nd pl. gen. as possessive)

passages.title: Iōhannes 10:1–10 · Ego Sum Ostium Ovium
passages.latin: Amen, amen dico vobis: qui non intrat per ostium in ovile ovium, sed ascendit aliunde, ille fur est et latro. Qui autem intrat per ostium, pastor est ovium. Huic ostiarius aperit, et oves vocem eius audiunt, et proprias oves vocat nominatim et educit eas. Et cum proprias oves emiserit, ante eas vadit, et oves illum sequuntur, quia sciunt vocem eius. Alienum autem non sequuntur, sed fugiunt ab eo, quia non noverunt vocem alienorum. Hoc proverbium dixit eis Iesus; illi autem non cognoverunt quid loqueretur eis. Dixit ergo eis iterum Iesus: Amen, amen dico vobis, quia ego sum ostium ovium. Omnes quotquot venerunt, fures sunt et latrones; sed non audierunt eos oves. Ego sum ostium; per me si quis introierit, salvabitur, et ingredietur, et egredietur, et pascua inveniet. Fur non venit nisi ut furetur, et mactet, et perdat. Ego veni ut vitam habeant et abundantius habeant.
passages.english: Amen, amen I say to you: He that entereth not by the door into the sheepfold, but climbeth up another way, the same is a thief and a robber. But he that entereth in by the door is the shepherd of the sheep. To him the porter openeth; and the sheep hear his voice, and he calleth his own sheep by name, and leadeth them out. And when he hath let out his own sheep, he goeth before them: and the sheep follow him, because they know his voice. But a stranger they follow not, but fly from him, because they know not the voice of strangers. This proverb Jesus spoke to them; but they understood not what he spoke to them. Jesus therefore said to them again: Amen, amen I say to you, I am the door of the sheep. All others, as many as have come, are thieves and robbers; and the sheep heard them not. I am the door. By me, if any man enter in, he shall be saved, and shall go in, and go out, and shall find pastures. The thief cometh not, but for to steal, and to kill, and to destroy. I am come that they may have life, and may have it more abundantly.
passages.notes: The Good Shepherd discourse begins. Focus: perfect tense of 2nd conjugation verbs and 3rd declension adjectives. Cognoverunt (perf. of cognosco), audierunt (perf. of audio), venerunt (perf. of venio). Proprias (3rd decl. adj.).

sentences: Ego sum ostium ovium.
Oves vocem eius audiunt et proprias oves vocat nominatim.
Ego veni ut vitam habeant et abundantius habeant.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Circle every perfect tense verb. Underline every 3rd-declension adjective. Draw a box around the noun it modifies.

parsing.answer: cognosco, cognoscere — perfect active indicative, 3rd person plural; 3rd conjugation; 'they knew/recognized'
audio, audire — perfect active indicative, 3rd person plural; 4th conjugation; 'heard'
proprius, -a, -um — feminine accusative plural; agrees with oves (ovis, f., acc. pl.)
alienus, -a, -um — masculine accusative singular; 3rd-declension style adj.; 'a stranger'

identification.instruction: Find all perfect tense verbs in John 10:1–10 and give their infinitive, conjugation number, and translation.
identification.answer: cognoverunt (cognosco, 3rd, 'knew'); audierunt (audio, 4th, 'heard'); venerunt (venio, 4th, 'came'); noverunt (nosco, 3rd, 'knew' — alternate perfect); emiserit (emitto, 3rd — perfect subjunctive)

passages.title: Iōhannes 11:17–27 · Ego Sum Resurrectiō
passages.latin: Venit itaque Iesus et invenit eum quatuor dies iam in monumento habentem. Erat autem Bethania iuxta Hierosolymam quasi stadiis quindecim. Multi autem ex Iudaeis venerant ad Martham et Mariam, ut consolarentur eas de fratre earum. Martha ergo ut audivit quia Iesus venit, occurrit illi; Maria autem domi sedebat. Dixit ergo Martha ad Iesum: Domine, si fuisses hic, frater meus non fuisset mortuus. Sed et nunc scio quia quaecumque poposceris a Deo, dabit tibi Deus. Dicit ei Iesus: Resurget frater tuus. Dicit ei Martha: Scio quia resurget in resurrectione in novissimo die. Dixit ei Iesus: Ego sum resurrectio et vita. Qui credit in me, etiam si mortuus fuerit, vivet. Et omnis qui vivit et credit in me, non morietur in aeternum. Credis hoc? Ait illi: Utique, Domine; ego credidi quia tu es Christus Filius Dei vivi, qui in hunc mundum venisti.
passages.english: Jesus therefore came, and found that he had been four days already in the grave. Now Bethania was near Jerusalem, about fifteen furlongs off. And many of the Jews were come to Martha and Mary, to comfort them concerning their brother. Martha therefore, as soon as she heard that Jesus was come, went to meet him: but Mary sat at home. Martha therefore said to Jesus: Lord, if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died. But now also I know that whatsoever thou wilt ask of God, God will give it thee. Jesus saith to her: Thy brother shall rise again. Martha saith to him: I know that he shall rise again, in the resurrection at the last day. Jesus said to her: I am the resurrection and the life. He that believeth in me, although he be dead, shall live. And every one that liveth, and believeth in me, shall not die for ever. Believest thou this? She saith to him: Yea, Lord, I have believed that thou art Christ the Son of the living God, who art come into this world.
passages.notes: Martha's confession before the raising of Lazarus. Review passage: adjectives, pronouns, perfect, 3rd decl. adjectives. Resurrectio, -onis — 3rd decl. f. Vivus, -a, -um — living, alive (3rd decl. adjective pattern in Dei vivi).

sentences: Ego sum resurrectio et vita.
Qui credit in me etiam si mortuus fuerit vivet.
Tu es Christus Filius Dei vivi.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify every pronoun (personal or relative). Label the case of each noun and adjective in sentence 3.

parsing.answer: resurgo, resurgere — future active indicative, 3rd person singular; 3rd conjugation; 'will rise again'
credo, credere — perfect active indicative, 1st person singular; 'I have believed, I believed'
vivus, -a, -um — masculine genitive singular; agrees with Dei (Deus, masc.); 'of the living [God]'
mortuus, -a, -um — masculine nominative singular; perfect passive participle of morior used as adjective; 'dead'

identification.instruction: Find all personal and demonstrative pronouns in John 11:17–27. List form, case, and what each refers to.
identification.answer: Iesus (noun, not pronoun); illi (dat. sg. — to him/her); eas (acc. pl. — them, Martha and Mary); ei (dat. sg. — to her, Martha); ego (nom. sg. — Martha speaking, emphatic); tu (nom. sg. — Jesus); hoc (neut. acc. sg. — this thing)

passages.title: Iōhannes 15:1–8 · Ego Sum Vītis Vēra
passages.latin: Ego sum vitis vera, et Pater meus agricola est. Omnem palmitem in me non ferentem fructum tollet eum; et omnem qui fert fructum, purgabit eum, ut fructum plus afferat. Iam vos mundi estis propter sermonem quem locutus sum vobis. Manete in me et ego in vobis. Sicut palmes non potest ferre fructum a semetipso, nisi manserit in vite, sic nec vos, nisi in me manseritis. Ego sum vitis, vos palmites. Qui manet in me et ego in eo, hic fert fructum multum; quia sine me nihil potestis facere. Si quis in me non manserit, mittetur foras ut palmes, et arescet; et colligent eum, et in ignem mittent, et ardet. Si manseritis in me et verba mea in vobis manserint, quodcumque volueritis, petetis, et fiet vobis. In hoc clarificatus est Pater meus, ut fructum plurimum feratis.
passages.english: I am the true vine; and my Father is the husbandman. Every branch in me, that beareth not fruit, he will take away: and every one that beareth fruit, he will purge it, that it may bring forth more fruit. Now you are clean by reason of the word, which I have spoken to you. Abide in me, and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit of itself, unless it abide in the vine, so neither can you, unless you abide in me. I am the vine; you are the branches: he that abideth in me, and I in him, the same beareth much fruit: for without me you can do nothing. If any one abide not in me, he shall be cast forth as a branch, and shall wither, and they shall gather him up, and cast him into the fire, and he burneth. If you abide in me, and my words abide in you, you shall ask whatever you will, and it shall be done unto you. In this is my Father glorified; that you bring forth very much fruit.
passages.notes: The True Vine. Focus: passive voice. Mittetur foras (will be cast out) — future passive. Purgabit (will cleanse) — active. Clarificatus est (has been glorified) — perfect passive. Note the contrast of active and passive forms.

sentences: Ego sum vitis vera, et Pater meus agricola est.
Si quis in me non manserit, mittetur foras ut palmes.
Sine me nihil potestis facere.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Underline every passive voice verb. Circle every active voice verb. Write A or P above each verb.

parsing.answer: mitto, mittere — future passive indicative, 3rd person singular; 3rd conjugation; 'will be cast/thrown'

parsing.prompt: Parse: clarificatus est (in 'clarificatus est Pater meus')
parsing.answer: clarifico, clarificare — perfect passive indicative, 3rd person singular; 1st conjugation; 'has been glorified'
tollo, tollere — future active indicative, 3rd person singular; 3rd conjugation; 'will take away'
maneo, manere — present active imperative, 2nd person plural; 2nd conjugation; 'Remain! Abide!'

identification.instruction: List all passive voice verbs in John 15:1–8. Give tense, person, and number for each.
identification.answer: mittetur (fut. pass., 3rd sg.); clarificatus est (perf. pass., 3rd sg.); purgabit is active; mundi estis — mundi is an adjective ('clean'), not passive; colligent is active

passages.title: Iōhannes 20:19–23 · Accipite Spīritum Sānctum
passages.latin: Cum ergo sero esset die illo, una sabbatorum, et fores essent clausae ubi erant discipuli congregati propter metum Iudaeorum, venit Iesus et stetit in medio et dicit eis: Pax vobis. Et cum hoc dixisset, ostendit eis manus et latus. Gavisi sunt ergo discipuli, viso Domino. Dixit ergo eis iterum: Pax vobis. Sicut misit me Pater, et ego mitto vos. Haec cum dixisset, insufflavit et dicit eis: Accipite Spiritum Sanctum. Quorum remiseritis peccata, remittuntur eis; et quorum retinueritis, retenta sunt.
passages.english: Now when it was late that same day, the first of the week, and the doors were shut, where the disciples were gathered together, for fear of the Jews, Jesus came and stood in the midst, and said to them: Peace be to you. And when he had said this, he shewed them his hands and his side. The disciples therefore were glad, when they saw the Lord. He said therefore to them again: Peace be to you. As the Father hath sent me, I also send you. When he had said this, he breathed on them; and he said to them: Receive ye the Holy Ghost. Whose sins you shall forgive, they are forgiven them; and whose sins you shall retain, they are retained.
passages.notes: The first appearance to the disciples; gift of the Holy Spirit. Focus: 4th declension nouns. Manus, -us (hand) — 4th decl. fem. Spiritus, -us (spirit) — 4th decl. masc. Also: latus, -eris (side) — 3rd decl. neuter.

sentences: Pax vobis.
Accipite Spiritum Sanctum.
Ostendit eis manus et latus.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Circle every 4th-declension noun. Write its case and number above it. (Manus and Spiritus are both 4th declension.)

parsing.answer: manus, -us, f. — accusative plural; 4th declension; direct object of ostendit
spiritus, -us, m. — accusative singular; 4th declension; direct object of Accipite
sanctus, -a, -um — masculine accusative singular; agrees with Spiritum (masc. acc. sg.)
remitto, remittere — present passive indicative, 3rd person plural; 'are forgiven, are sent back'

identification.instruction: Find all 4th-declension nouns in John 20:19–23. List each with its case, number, and gender.
identification.answer: manus (acc. pl., f. — hands); Spiritum (acc. sg., m. — Spirit). Note: Spiritus appears in nominative form implicitly as a 4th decl. noun; fores, -ium (doors) is actually 3rd decl. f.

passages.title: Iōhannes 19:16–20, 25–30 · Cōnsummātum Est
passages.latin: Tunc ergo tradidit eis illum ut crucifigeretur. Susceperunt autem Iesum et eduxerunt. Et baiulans sibi crucem exivit in eum qui dicitur Calvariae locum, Hebraice autem Golgotha, ubi crucifixerunt eum. Scripsit autem et titulum Pilatus et posuit super crucem. Erat autem scriptum: IESUS NAZARENUS REX IUDAEORUM. Stabant autem iuxta crucem Iesu mater eius et soror matris eius, Maria Cleophae, et Maria Magdalene. Cum vidisset ergo Iesus matrem et discipulum stantem quem diligebat, dicit matri suae: Mulier, ecce filius tuus. Deinde dicit discipulo: Ecce mater tua. Et ex illa hora accepit eam discipulus in sua. Postea sciens Iesus quia omnia consummata sunt, ut consummaretur Scriptura, dixit: Sitio. Cum ergo accepisset Iesus acetum, dixit: Consummatum est. Et inclinato capite tradidit spiritum.
passages.english: Then therefore he delivered him to them to be crucified. And they took Jesus, and led him forth. And bearing his own cross, he went forth to that place which is called Calvary, but in Hebrew, Golgotha. Where they crucified him. And Pilate wrote a title also, and he put it upon the cross. And the writing was: JESUS OF NAZARETH, THE KING OF THE JEWS. Now there stood by the cross of Jesus, his mother, and his mother's sister, Mary of Cleophas, and Mary Magdalen. When Jesus therefore had seen his mother and the disciple standing whom he loved, he saith to his mother: Woman, behold thy son. After that, he saith to the disciple: Behold thy mother. And from that hour, the disciple took her to his own. Afterwards, Jesus knowing that all things were now accomplished, that the scripture might be fulfilled, said: I thirst. Jesus therefore, when he had taken the vinegar, said: It is consummated. And bowing his head, he gave up the ghost.
passages.notes: The Crucifixion. Focus: perfect passive tense. Erat scriptum (was written), crucifixerunt (crucified), consummatum est (it is finished/consummated). The perfect passive participle + sum formula.

vocab_hints.english: it is finished, it is consummated

sentences: Erat autem scriptum: IESUS NAZARENUS REX IUDAEORUM.
Consummatum est.
Et inclinato capite tradidit spiritum.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Underline every perfect passive construction (participle + form of sum). Circle the participle. Draw a box around the form of sum.

parsing.answer: scribo, scribere — perfect passive participle, neuter nominative singular; with erat = pluperfect passive 'it had been written'
consummo, consummare — perfect passive indicative, 3rd person singular; 'it has been completed/finished'
crucifigo, crucifigere — perfect active indicative, 3rd person plural; 3rd conjugation; 'they crucified'
trado, tradere — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular; 3rd conjugation; 'gave up, handed over'

identification.instruction: Find all perfect passive constructions (participle + esse) in John 19:16–30. List each and translate.
identification.answer: erat scriptum (pluperfect passive — 'had been written'); consummata sunt (perf. pass. — 'have been accomplished'); consummatum est (perf. pass. — 'it is finished'); tradidit spiritum is active, not passive

passages.title: Iōhannes 5:19–30 · Quae Pater Facit Haec et Fīlius Facit
passages.latin: Respondit ergo Iesus et dixit eis: Amen, amen dico vobis, non potest Filius a semetipso facere quidquam, nisi quod viderit Patrem facientem; quaecumque enim ille facit, haec et Filius similiter facit. Pater enim diligit Filium et omnia demonstrat ei quae ipse facit; et maiora his demonstrabit ei opera, ut vos miremini. Sicut enim Pater suscitat mortuos et vivificat, sic et Filius quos vult vivificat. Neque enim Pater iudicat quemquam, sed omne iudicium dedit Filio; ut omnes honorificent Filium sicut honorificant Patrem. Qui non honorificat Filium, non honorificat Patrem qui misit illum. Amen, amen dico vobis quia qui verbum meum audit, et credit ei qui misit me, habet vitam aeternam, et in iudicium non venit, sed transiit a morte in vitam. Amen, amen dico vobis, quia venit hora, et nunc est, quando mortui audient vocem Filii Dei; et qui audierint, vivent. Sicut enim Pater habet vitam in semetipso, sic dedit et Filio vitam habere in semetipso. Non possum ego a meipso facere quidquam.
passages.english: Then Jesus answered, and said to them: Amen, amen I say unto you, the Son cannot do any thing of himself, but what he seeth the Father doing: for what things soever he doth, these the Son also doth in like manner. For the Father loveth the Son, and sheweth him all things which himself doth: and greater works than these will he shew him, that you may wonder. For as the Father raiseth up the dead, and giveth life: so the Son also giveth life to whom he will. For neither doth the Father judge any man, but hath given all judgment to the Son; That all men may honour the Son, as they honour the Father. He who honoureth not the Son, honoureth not the Father, who hath sent him. Amen, amen I say unto you, that he who heareth my word, and believeth him that sent me, hath life everlasting; and cometh not into judgment, but is passed from death to life. I cannot of myself do any thing.
passages.notes: Jesus' testimony about himself and the Father. Focus: possum (to be able). Non potest Filius... non possum ego — possum with infinitive. Also dedit (gave — perfect of do), habet (has — present of habeo).

sentences: Non potest Filius a semetipso facere quidquam.
Qui verbum meum audit et credit ei qui misit me, habet vitam aeternam.
Non possum ego a meipso facere quidquam.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Circle every form of possum. Underline its complementary infinitive. Translate each possum phrase.

parsing.answer: possum, posse — present active indicative, 3rd person singular; 'is able, can'
possum, posse — present active indicative, 1st person singular; 'I am able, I can'
facio, facere — present active infinitive; 3rd conjugation -io; complementary infinitive with possum

parsing.prompt: Parse: transiit (in 'transiit a morte in vitam')
parsing.answer: transeo, transire — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular; 'has passed, crossed over'

identification.instruction: Find all uses of possum (or potest, possunt, etc.) in John 5:19–30. For each, identify the subject and the complementary infinitive.
identification.answer: non potest Filius facere (subject: Filius; infinitive: facere); non possum ego facere (subject: ego/Jesus; infinitive: facere)

passages.title: Iōhannes 20:24–31 · Dominus Meus et Deus Meus
passages.latin: Thomas autem, unus ex duodecim, qui dicitur Didymus, non erat cum eis quando venit Iesus. Dixerunt ergo ei alii discipuli: Vidimus Dominum. Ille autem dixit eis: Nisi videro in manibus eius figuram clavorum et mittam digitum meum in figuram clavorum et mittam manum meam in latus eius, non credam. Et post dies octo iterum erant discipuli eius intus et Thomas cum eis. Venit Iesus ianuis clausis et stetit in medio et dixit: Pax vobis. Deinde dicit Thomae: Infer digitum tuum huc et vide manus meas, et adfer manum tuam et mitte in latus meum; et noli esse incredulus, sed fidelis. Respondit Thomas et dixit ei: Dominus meus et Deus meus. Dicit ei Iesus: Quia vidisti me Thoma, credidisti; beati qui non viderunt et crediderunt. Multa quidem et alia signa fecit Iesus in conspectu discipulorum suorum quae non sunt scripta in libro hoc. Haec autem scripta sunt ut credatis quia Iesus est Christus Filius Dei, et ut credentes vitam habeatis in nomine eius.
passages.english: Now Thomas, one of the twelve, who is called Didymus, was not with them when Jesus came. The other disciples therefore said to him: We have seen the Lord. But he said to them: Except I shall see in his hands the print of the nails, and put my finger into the place of the nails, and put my hand into his side, I will not believe. And after eight days again his disciples were within, and Thomas with them. Jesus cometh, the doors being shut, and stood in the midst, and said: Peace be to you. Then he saith to Thomas: Put in thy finger hither, and see my hands; and bring hither thy hand, and put it into my side; and be not faithless, but believing. Thomas answered, and said to him: My Lord and my God. Jesus saith to him: Because thou hast seen me, Thomas, thou hast believed: blessed are they that have not seen, and have believed. Many other signs also did Jesus in the sight of his disciples, which are not written in this book. But these are written, that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God: and that believing, you may have life in his name.
passages.notes: Thomas's confession — the climax of the Gospel of John. Final review: passive voice, 4th/5th declension, possum, all grammar from the course. Haec scripta sunt — perfect passive. Credatis — present subjunctive (purpose clause).

sentences: Dominus meus et Deus meus.
Beati qui non viderunt et crediderunt.
Haec autem scripta sunt ut credatis quia Iesus est Christus Filius Dei.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 3, identify: (1) the perfect passive construction, (2) the purpose clause, and (3) the indirect statement. Label each in the margin.

parsing.prompt: Parse: scripta sunt (in 'haec autem scripta sunt')
parsing.answer: scribo, scribere — perfect passive indicative, 3rd person plural; neuter pl. subject (haec); 'these things have been written'
credo, credere — present active subjunctive, 2nd person plural; purpose clause with ut; 'that you may believe'
video, videre — perfect active indicative, 3rd person plural; 2nd conjugation; 'they saw'
credo, credere — perfect active indicative, 3rd person plural; 3rd conjugation; 'they believed'

identification.instruction: John 20:31 is the stated purpose of the whole Gospel. Identify every grammatical construction in this verse: Haec autem scripta sunt ut credatis quia Iesus est Christus Filius Dei, et ut credentes vitam habeatis in nomine eius.
identification.answer: Haec scripta sunt — perfect passive (subject: haec, neuter pl.); ut credatis — purpose clause (present subjunctive); quia Iesus est Christus — indirect statement (quia + indicative, Latin idiom); Filius Dei — predicate nominative; ut ... habeatis — second purpose clause; credentes — present participle ('believing ones'); in nomine eius — ablative of means/manner

passages.title: I Iōhannes 1:1–4 · Quod Erat Ab Initiō
passages.latin: Quod erat ab initio, quod audivimus, quod vidimus oculis nostris, quod perspeximus et manus nostrae contrectaverunt de Verbo vitae; et vita manifestata est, et vidimus et testes sumus et annuntiamus vobis vitam aeternam, quae erat apud Patrem et apparuit nobis. Quod vidimus et audivimus, annuntiamus vobis, ut et vos societatem habeatis nobiscum. Et societas nostra sit cum Patre et cum Filio eius Iesu Christo. Et haec scribimus vobis ut gaudium vestrum sit plenum.
passages.english: That which was from the beginning, which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, which we have looked upon, and our hands have handled, of the word of life. For the life was manifested: and we have seen and do bear witness, and declare unto you the life, eternal life, which was with the Father, and hath appeared to us. That which we have seen and have heard, we declare unto you, that you also may have fellowship with us, and our fellowship may be with the Father, and with his Son Jesus Christ. And these things we write to you, that you may rejoice, and your joy may be full.
passages.notes: The prologue of 1 John. Third and fourth conjugation verbs (present system). Audivimus, vidimus, annuntiamus — review of perfect and present active indicative.

sentences: Quod audivimus, quod vidimus oculis nostris, annuntiamus vobis.
Et haec scribimus vobis ut gaudium vestrum sit plenum.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Mark every 3rd or 4th conjugation verb with its conjugation number.

parsing.answer: audio, audire — perfect active indicative, 1st person plural, 4th conjugation; 'we heard/have heard'
annuntio, annuntiare — present active indicative, 1st person plural, 1st conjugation; 'we announce'
scribo, scribere — present active indicative, 1st person plural, 3rd conjugation; 'we write'
appareo, apparere — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular, 2nd conjugation; 'appeared, has appeared'

identification.instruction: Sort the verbs in 1 John 1:1–4 by conjugation (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th). List each under the correct heading.
identification.answer: 1st: annuntiamus, scribimus... Wait — scribo is 3rd conj. | 1st conj: annuntiamus; 2nd conj: vidimus (video), apparuit (appareo); 3rd conj: scribo; 4th conj: audivimus (audio); irregular: erat (sum)

passages.title: I Iōhannes 1:5–10 · Deus Lūx Est
passages.latin: Et haec est annuntiatio quam audivimus ab eo et annuntiamus vobis: quia Deus lux est, et tenebrae in eo non sunt ullae. Si dixerimus quoniam societatem habemus cum eo et in tenebris ambulamus, mentimur et veritatem non facimus. Si autem in luce ambulamus, sicut et ipse est in luce, societatem habemus ad invicem, et sanguis Iesu Filii eius mundat nos ab omni peccato. Si dixerimus quoniam peccatum non habemus, ipsi nos seducimus et veritas in nobis non est. Si confitemur peccata nostra, fidelis est et iustus ut remittat nobis peccata nostra et emundet nos ab omni iniquitate. Si dixerimus quoniam non peccavimus, mendacem facimus eum et verbum eius non est in nobis.
passages.english: And this is the declaration which we have heard from him, and declare unto you: That God is light, and in him there is no darkness at all. If we say that we have fellowship with him, and walk in darkness, we lie, and do not the truth. But if we walk in the light, as he also is in the light, we have fellowship one with another, and the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin. If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us. If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just, to forgive us our sins, and to cleanse us from all iniquity. If we say that we have not sinned, we make him a liar, and his word is not in us.
passages.notes: The light/darkness contrast. Perfect system active (all four conjugations). Dixerimus — future perfect active. Mundat — 3rd conjugation present.

sentences: Deus lux est, et tenebrae in eo non sunt ullae.
Si confitemur peccata nostra, fidelis est et iustus ut remittat nobis peccata nostra.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify every verb tense. Circle every conditional (si) clause.

parsing.answer: dico, dicere — future perfect active indicative (or perfect active subjunctive), 1st person plural; 3rd conjugation; 'if we shall have said'
mundo, mundare — present active indicative, 3rd person singular, 1st conjugation; 'cleanses'
confiteor, confiteri — present indicative (deponent), 1st person plural, 2nd conjugation; 'we confess'
remitto, remittere — present active subjunctive, 3rd person singular; purpose clause with ut; 'that he may forgive'

identification.instruction: Find all conditional (si) clauses in 1 John 1:5–10. How many are there? What verb form follows si in each?
identification.answer: 4 conditional clauses (si dixerimus × 3, si confitemur); dixerimus = future perfect (or perf. subjunctive); confitemur = present indicative. Pattern: si + perf./fut.perf. → simple condition.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 2:1–6 · Advocātus Apud Patrem
passages.latin: Filioli mei, haec scribo vobis ut non peccetis. Sed et si quis peccaverit, advocatum habemus apud Patrem Iesum Christum iustum; et ipse est propitiatio pro peccatis nostris; non pro nostris autem tantum, sed etiam pro totius mundi. Et in hoc scimus quoniam cognovimus eum, si mandata eius observamus. Qui dicit quoniam novit eum, et mandata eius non observat, mendax est, et in hoc veritas non est. Qui autem servat verbum eius, vere in hoc caritas Dei perfecta est. In hoc scimus quoniam in ipso sumus. Qui dicit se in ipso manere, debet, sicut ille ambulavit, et ipse ambulare.
passages.english: My little children, these things I write to you, that you may not sin. But if any man sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the just. And he is the propitiation for our sins: and not for ours only, but also for those of the whole world. And by this we know that we have known him, if we keep his commandments. He who saith that he knoweth him, and keepeth not his commandments, is a liar, and the truth is not in him. But he that keepeth his word, in him the charity of God is truly perfected: and by this we know that we are in him. He that saith he abideth in him, ought himself also to walk, even as he walked.
passages.notes: Christ as Advocate. Participle review: mandata observans, ambulavit. Debet (ought) + infinitive. Cognovimus, novit — different perfect forms of same root (cognosco vs. nosco).

sentences: Advocatum habemus apud Patrem Iesum Christum iustum.
Qui dicit quoniam novit eum et mandata eius non observat, mendax est.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Underline every participle. Circle every relative clause (qui, quae, quod + verb).

parsing.answer: pecco, peccare — present active subjunctive, 2nd person plural; 1st conjugation; purpose clause with ut
observo, observare — present active indicative, 1st person plural, 1st conjugation; 'we keep, observe'
debeo, debere — present active indicative, 3rd person singular, 2nd conjugation; 'ought, owes'
ambulo, ambulare — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular, 1st conjugation; 'walked'

identification.instruction: Find all present active participles in 1 John 2:1–6. List each with the noun it modifies.
identification.answer: No present active participles appear directly in these verses. The key forms are indicative and subjunctive. Note: mandata (commandments) is a noun, not a participle of mando here.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 2:7–11 · Mandātum Novum
passages.latin: Carissimi, non mandatum novum scribo vobis, sed mandatum vetus, quod habuistis ab initio; mandatum vetus est verbum quod audistis. Iterum mandatum novum scribo vobis, quod verum est et in ipso et in vobis, quia tenebrae transierunt et lux vera iam lucet. Qui dicit se in luce esse et fratrem suum odit, in tenebris est adhuc. Qui diligit fratrem suum, in luce manet et scandalum in eo non est. Qui autem odit fratrem suum, in tenebris est et in tenebris ambulat et nescit quo eat, quia tenebrae obcaecaverunt oculos eius.
passages.english: Dearly beloved, I write not a new commandment to you, but an old commandment which you had from the beginning. The old commandment is the word which you have heard. Again I write you a new commandment, which is true both in him and in you; because the darkness is passed, and the true light now shineth. He that saith he is in the light, and hateth his brother, is in darkness even until now. He that loveth his brother, abideth in the light, and there is no scandal in him. But he that hateth his brother, is in darkness, and walketh in darkness, and knoweth not whither he goeth: because the darkness hath blinded his eyes.
passages.notes: The old and new commandment. Irregular verbs: eo (to go) — quo eat (where he goes). Participles: obcaecaverunt. Review of 3rd-4th conjugation imperfect/perfect.

sentences: Qui diligit fratrem suum in luce manet.
Qui autem odit fratrem suum in tenebris ambulat et nescit quo eat.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify the relative clause in each (qui + verb). Parse the main verb of each relative clause.

parsing.answer: odi, odisse — perfect active (used as present), 3rd person singular; defective verb; 'hates'
diligo, diligere — present active indicative, 3rd person singular, 3rd conjugation; 'loves'
eo, ire — present active subjunctive, 3rd person singular; indirect question; 'where he is going'
luceo, lucere — present active indicative, 3rd person singular, 2nd conjugation; 'shines'

identification.instruction: Find every relative clause (qui/quae/quod + verb) in 1 John 2:7–11. List the relative pronoun, its antecedent, and the verb in the clause.
identification.answer: quod habuistis (antecedent: mandatum); quod audistis (antecedent: verbum); quod verum est (antecedent: mandatum); Qui dicit... odit (antecedent: person); Qui diligit (antecedent: person); Qui autem odit (antecedent: person)

passages.title: I Iōhannes 2:12–17 · Nōlīte Dīligere Mundum
passages.latin: Scribo vobis, filioli, quoniam remittuntur vobis peccata propter nomen eius. Scribo vobis, patres, quoniam cognovistis eum qui ab initio est. Scribo vobis, iuvenes, quoniam vicistis malignum. Nolite diligere mundum, neque ea quae in mundo sunt. Si quis diligit mundum, non est caritas Patris in eo; quoniam omne quod est in mundo concupiscentia carnis est et concupiscentia oculorum et superbia vitae, quae non est ex Patre, sed ex mundo est. Et mundus transit et concupiscentia eius; qui autem facit voluntatem Dei, manet in aeternum.
passages.english: I write unto you, little children, because your sins are forgiven you for his name's sake. I write unto you, fathers, because you have known him, who is from the beginning. I write unto you, young men, because you have overcome the wicked one. Love not the world, nor the things which are in the world. If any man love the world, the charity of the Father is not in him. For all that is in the world, is the concupiscence of the flesh, and the concupiscence of the eyes, and the pride of life, which is not of the Father, but is of the world. And the world passeth away, and the concupiscence thereof: but he that doth the will of God, abideth for ever.
passages.notes: Do not love the world. Nolite + infinitive = negative command. Vicistis — perfect of vinco. Manet in aeternum — present of maneo.

vocab_hints.english: to not want; nolite + inf. = do not!

sentences: Nolite diligere mundum neque ea quae in mundo sunt.
Qui autem facit voluntatem Dei manet in aeternum.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify the negative command form. Parse the main verb of each sentence.

parsing.answer: nolo, nolle — present active imperative, 2nd person plural; 'Do not! Refuse to!' (negative command with infinitive)
diligo, diligere — present active infinitive; 3rd conjugation; complementary infinitive with nolite
vinco, vincere — perfect active indicative, 2nd person plural, 3rd conjugation; 'you have conquered/overcome'
transeo, transire — present active indicative, 3rd person singular; compound of eo; 'passes away'

identification.instruction: John addresses three groups in 2:12–14. Identify the three groups and the verb tense used for each claim. Why might these tenses differ?
identification.answer: filioli (little children) — remittuntur, present passive; patres (fathers) — cognovistis, perfect (completed knowledge); iuvenes (young men) — vicistis, perfect (completed victory). The perfect tense for fathers/youth emphasizes accomplished facts.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 2:18–25 · Antīchristus
passages.latin: Filioli, novissima hora est, et sicut audistis quia antichristus venit, et nunc antichristi multi facti sunt: unde scimus quia novissima hora est. Ex nobis prodierunt, sed non erant ex nobis; nam si fuissent ex nobis, permansissent utique nobiscum; sed ut manifesti fierent quoniam non sunt omnes ex nobis. Et vos unctionem habetis a Sancto, et nostis omnia. Non scripsi vobis quasi ignorantibus veritatem, sed quasi scientibus eam, et quoniam omne mendacium ex veritate non est. Quis est mendax nisi is qui negat quoniam Iesus non est Christus? Hic est antichristus, qui negat Patrem et Filium. Omnis qui negat Filium nec Patrem habet. Qui confitetur Filium et Patrem habet. Vos quod audistis ab initio in vobis maneat.
passages.english: Little children, it is the last hour; and as you have heard that Antichrist cometh, even now there are become many Antichrists: whereby we know that it is the last hour. They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would no doubt have remained with us; but that they may be made manifest, that they are not all of us. But you have the unction from the Holy One, and know all things. I have not written to you as to them that know not the truth, but as to them that know it, and because no lie is of the truth. Who is a liar, but he who denieth that Jesus is not the Christ? This is Antichrist, who denieth the Father, and the Son.
passages.notes: Warning about antichrists. Subjunctive mood: fierent (imperfect passive subj.), permansissent (pluperfect subj. — past contrary-to-fact condition). Key subjunctive usage.

sentences: Quis est mendax nisi is qui negat quoniam Iesus non est Christus?
Qui confitetur Filium et Patrem habet.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify any subjunctive verbs. Label each as purpose, result, condition, or indirect question.

parsing.answer: fio, fieri — imperfect passive subjunctive, 3rd person plural; purpose clause; 'that they might be made'
permaneo, permanere — pluperfect active subjunctive, 3rd person plural; past contrary-to-fact condition; 'they would have remained'
sum, esse — pluperfect active subjunctive, 3rd person plural; condition (si clause); 'if they had been'
nego, negare — present active indicative, 3rd person singular, 1st conjugation; 'denies'

identification.instruction: Identify the past contrary-to-fact condition in 1 John 2:18–25 (si... clause). What form is in the si clause? What form is in the main clause?
identification.answer: si fuissent ex nobis, permansissent utique nobiscum — si clause: fuissent (pluperfect subjunctive); main clause: permansissent (pluperfect subjunctive). This is a past contrary-to-fact condition: 'if they had been [they weren't], they would have remained [they didn't].'

passages.title: I Iōhannes 2:26–29 · Manet in Vōbīs Unctio
passages.latin: Haec scripsi vobis de his qui seducunt vos. Et vos unctionem quam accepistis ab eo, maneat in vobis. Et non necesse habetis ut aliquis doceat vos, sed sicut unctio eius docet vos de omnibus et verum est et non est mendacium, et sicut docuit vos, manete in eo. Et nunc, filioli, manete in eo, ut cum apparuerit, habeamus fiduciam et non confundamur ab eo in adventu eius. Si scitis quoniam iustus est, scitote quoniam et omnis qui facit iustitiam ex ipso natus est.
passages.english: These things have I written to you, concerning them that seduce you. And as for you, let the unction, which you have received from him, abide in you. And you have no need that any man teach you; but as his unction teacheth you of all things, and is truth, and is no lie; and as it hath taught you, abide in him. And now, little children, abide in him, that when he shall appear, we may have confidence, and not be confounded by him at his coming. If you know that he is just, know ye, that every one also who doth justice, is born of him.
passages.notes: The anointing as teacher. Indirect statement with infinitive: necesse habetis ut doceat — indirect command (subjunctive). Apparuerit — future perfect. Natus est — perfect passive.

vocab_hints.english: has been born (perf. pass. of nascor)

sentences: Manete in eo ut cum apparuerit habeamus fiduciam.
Omnis qui facit iustitiam ex ipso natus est.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Circle the subjunctive verb. Identify whether it is purpose, result, or temporal (cum clause).

parsing.answer: doceo, docere — present active subjunctive, 3rd person singular, 2nd conjugation; indirect command with ut; 'that someone teach'
appareo, apparere — future perfect active indicative (or perfect subjunctive), 3rd person singular, 2nd conjugation; temporal clause; 'when he shall have appeared'
confundo, confundere — present passive subjunctive, 1st person plural; 'that we may not be put to shame'
nascor, nasci — perfect indicative (deponent), 3rd person singular; 'was born, has been born'

identification.instruction: Identify every subjunctive verb in 1 John 2:26–29. For each, name the clause type (purpose, indirect command, fear clause, cum clause, etc.).
identification.answer: doceat (ut + subj. — indirect command); habeamus (ut + subj. — purpose: 'that we may have'); confundamur (negative purpose: 'not be put to shame'); natus est is perfect deponent, not subjunctive

passages.title: I Iōhannes 3:1–3 · Fīliī Deī Sumus
passages.latin: Videte qualem caritatem dedit nobis Pater, ut filii Dei nominemur et simus. Propter hoc mundus non novit nos, quia non novit eum. Carissimi, nunc filii Dei sumus, et nondum apparuit quid erimus. Scimus quoniam cum apparuerit, similes ei erimus, quoniam videbimus eum sicuti est. Et omnis qui habet spem hanc in eo, sanctificat se, sicut et ille sanctus est.
passages.english: Behold what manner of charity the Father hath bestowed upon us, that we should be called, and should be the sons of God. Therefore the world knoweth us not, because it knew him not. Dearly beloved, we are now the sons of God; and it hath not yet appeared what we shall be. We know, that, when he shall appear, we shall be like to him: because we shall see him as he is. And every one that hath this hope in him, sanctifieth himself, as he also is holy.
passages.notes: We are children of God. Future tense: erimus (we shall be), videbimus (we shall see). Indirect statement: Scimus quoniam... similes ei erimus. Qualem — interrogative adjective.

sentences: Nunc filii Dei sumus.
Scimus quoniam cum apparuerit, similes ei erimus, quoniam videbimus eum sicuti est.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify the future tense verbs. Circle the indirect statement (quoniam clause). Mark the dative of comparison (similes + dative).

parsing.answer: sum, esse — future active indicative, 1st person plural; 'we shall be'
video, videre — future active indicative, 1st person plural, 2nd conjugation; 'we shall see'
nomino, nominare — present passive subjunctive, 1st person plural; purpose clause with ut; 'that we may be called'
sum, esse — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural; purpose clause with ut; 'that we may be'

identification.instruction: In 1 John 3:1–3, find all future tense verbs. List each with its subject. Why does John shift to the future tense here?
identification.answer: erimus (we = filii Dei — future: 'we shall be'); videbimus (we — 'we shall see'); erimus again in 'similes ei erimus'. The shift signals eschatological hope: what we are now (filii Dei) is confirmed; what we shall be fully is yet to be revealed.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 3:4–10 · Qui Facit Peccātum Ex Diabolō Est
passages.latin: Omnis qui facit peccatum et iniquitatem facit; et peccatum est iniquitas. Et scitis quia ille apparuit ut peccata nostra tolleret, et peccatum in eo non est. Omnis qui in eo manet non peccat, et omnis qui peccat non vidit eum nec cognovit eum. Filioli, nemo vos seducat. Qui facit iustitiam iustus est, sicut et ille iustus est. Qui facit peccatum ex diabolo est, quoniam ab initio diabolus peccat. In hoc apparuit Filius Dei, ut dissolvat opera diaboli. Omnis qui natus est ex Deo peccatum non facit, quoniam semen ipsius in eo manet, et non potest peccare, quoniam ex Deo natus est. In hoc manifesti sunt filii Dei et filii diaboli.
passages.english: Whosoever committeth sin committeth also iniquity; and sin is iniquity. And you know that he appeared to take away our sins, and sin is not in him. Whosoever abideth in him, sinneth not; and whosoever sinneth, hath not seen him, nor known him. Little children, let no man deceive you. He that doth justice is just, even as he is just. He that committeth sin is of the devil: for the devil sinneth from the beginning. For this purpose, the Son of God appeared, that he might destroy the works of the devil. Whosoever is born of God, committeth no sin: for his seed abideth in him, and he cannot sin, because he is born of God. In this the children of God are manifest, and the children of the devil.
passages.notes: On sin and sonship. Purpose infinitive: apparuit ut dissolvat (purpose subjunctive). Potest peccare — possum + infinitive. Non potest peccare — cannot sin.

sentences: In hoc apparuit Filius Dei ut dissolvat opera diaboli.
Non potest peccare quoniam ex Deo natus est.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, mark the purpose clause. In sentence 2, identify possum and its infinitive.

parsing.answer: dissolvo, dissolvere — present active subjunctive, 3rd person singular; purpose clause with ut; 'that he might destroy'
pecco, peccare — present active infinitive; 1st conjugation; complementary infinitive with potest

parsing.prompt: Parse: natus est (in 'ex Deo natus est')
parsing.answer: nascor, nasci (deponent) — perfect indicative, 3rd person singular; 'was born, has been born'
tollo, tollere — imperfect active subjunctive, 3rd person singular; purpose clause; 'that he might take away'

identification.instruction: Find all purpose clauses (ut + subjunctive) in 1 John 3:4–10. List each with the main verb and the subjunctive verb.
identification.answer: apparuit ut tolleret (main: apparuit; subj: tolleret — 'appeared in order to take away'); nemo vos seducat (jussive subj, not purpose); apparuit ut dissolvat (main: apparuit; subj: dissolvat — purpose)

passages.title: I Iōhannes 3:11–18 · Dīligāmus Nōn Verbō Sed Operē
passages.latin: Quoniam haec est annuntiatio quam audistis ab initio, ut diligamus invicem. Non sicut Cain qui ex maligno erat, et occidit fratrem suum. Et propter quid occidit eum?
