Quoniam opera eius maligna erant, fratris autem eius iusta. Nolite mirari, fratres, si odit vos mundus. Nos scimus quoniam translati sumus de morte in vitam, quoniam diligimus fratres. Qui non diligit, manet in morte. Omnis qui odit fratrem suum homicida est; et scitis quoniam omnis homicida non habet vitam aeternam in semetipso manentem. In hoc cognovimus caritatem Dei, quoniam ille animam suam pro nobis posuit; et nos debemus pro fratribus animas ponere. Qui habuerit substantiam huius mundi et viderit fratrem suum necessitatem habere et clauserit viscera sua ab eo, quomodo caritas Dei manet in eo? Filioli mei, non diligamus verbo neque lingua, sed opere et veritate.
passages.english: For this is the declaration which you have heard from the beginning, that you love one another. Not as Cain, who was of the wicked one, and killed his brother. And wherefore did he kill him? Because his own works were wicked, and his brother's just. Wonder not, brethren, if the world hate you. We know that we have passed from death to life, because we love the brethren. He that loveth not, abideth in death. Whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer. And you know that no murderer hath eternal life abiding in himself. In this we have known the charity of God, because he hath laid down his life for us: and we ought to lay down our lives for the brethren. He that hath the substance of this world, and shall see his brother in need, and shall shut up his bowels from him: how doth the charity of God abide in him? My little children, let us not love in word, nor in tongue, but in deed, and in truth.
passages.notes: Love in deed, not word. Ablative absolute: opera eius maligna erant, fratris autem eius iusta (comparison). Translati sumus — perfect passive. Debemus ponere — debeo + infinitive.

sentences: Non diligamus verbo neque lingua sed opere et veritate.
In hoc cognovimus caritatem Dei quoniam ille animam suam pro nobis posuit.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the hortatory subjunctive. In sentence 2, identify the indirect statement.

parsing.answer: diligo, diligere — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural; purpose clause or hortatory ('let us love')
transfero, transferre — perfect passive indicative, 1st person plural; 'we have been transferred/passed'
pono, ponere — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular, 3rd conjugation; 'laid down, placed'
debeo, debere — present active indicative, 1st person plural, 2nd conjugation; 'we ought, owe'

identification.instruction: Find the ablative absolute construction in 1 John 3:11–18 (or note its absence). Then identify any gerundive construction.
identification.answer: No true ablative absolute in this passage. However, note: the hortatory subjunctive non diligamus (let us not love) functions as an indirect command. The passage uses comparison (non sicut Cain) rather than ablative absolute for attendant circumstances.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 3:19–24 · In Hōc Scīmus
passages.latin: In hoc cognoscimus quoniam ex veritate sumus, et in conspectu eius suadebimus corda nostra. Quoniam si reprehenderit nos cor nostrum, maior est Deus corde nostro et novit omnia. Carissimi, si cor nostrum non reprehenderit nos, fiduciam habemus ad Deum; et quodcumque petierimus, accipiemus ab eo, quoniam mandata eius servamus et ea quae sunt placita coram illo facimus. Et hoc est mandatum eius, ut credamus in nomine Filii eius Iesu Christi, et diligamus alterutrum sicut dedit mandatum nobis. Et qui servat mandata eius, in illo manet et ipse in eo; et in hoc scimus quoniam manet in nobis, de Spiritu quem dedit nobis.
passages.english: In this we know that we are of the truth: and in his sight shall persuade our hearts. For if our heart reprehend us, God is greater than our heart, and knoweth all things. Dearly beloved, if our heart do not reprehend us, we have confidence towards God; and whatsoever we shall ask, we shall receive of him: because we keep his commandments, and do those things which are pleasing in his sight. And this is his commandment, that we should believe in the name of his Son Jesus Christ: and love one another, as he hath given commandment to us. And he that keepeth his commandments, abideth in him, and he in him. And in this we know that he abideth in us, by the Spirit which he hath given us.
passages.notes: Review passage. Indirect statement: in hoc scimus quoniam... Conditional: si cor non reprehenderit (future perfect in condition). Mandatum: ut credamus (indirect command with subjunctive).

sentences: Quodcumque petierimus accipiemus ab eo quoniam mandata eius servamus.
Et hoc est mandatum eius ut credamus in nomine Filii eius Iesu Christi.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify the indirect command (ut + subj.) in sentence 2. Identify the future perfect in sentence 1.

parsing.answer: credo, credere — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural; indirect command with ut; 'that we believe'
peto, petere — future perfect active indicative (or perfect subjunctive), 1st person plural, 3rd conjugation; 'whatever we shall have asked'
servo, servare — present active indicative, 1st person plural, 1st conjugation; 'we keep, guard'
reprehendo, reprehendere — future perfect active (or perfect subjunctive), 3rd person singular; conditional clause; 'if our heart shall have accused'

identification.instruction: Review: identify one example each of (a) indirect statement, (b) indirect command, (c) purpose clause, and (d) conditional clause in 1 John 3:19–24.
identification.answer: a) indirect statement: scimus quoniam manet in nobis (quoniam + indicative); b) indirect command: ut credamus... et diligamus (ut + subjunctive); c) purpose: not clearly present — nearest is the ut clause (could double as purpose); d) conditional: si cor nostrum non reprehenderit nos, fiduciam habemus (future perfect in si clause, present indicative in apodosis)

passages.title: I Iōhannes 4:1–6 · Probāte Spīritūs
passages.latin: Carissimi, nolite omni spiritui credere, sed probate spiritus si ex Deo sint, quoniam multi pseudoprophetae exierunt in mundum. In hoc cognoscitur Spiritus Dei: omnis spiritus qui confitetur Iesum Christum in carne venisse, ex Deo est. Et omnis spiritus qui solvit Iesum ex Deo non est, et hic est antichristus quem audistis quia venit, et nunc iam in mundo est. Vos ex Deo estis, filioli, et vicistis eum, quoniam maior est qui in vobis est quam qui in mundo. Ipsi de mundo sunt; ideo de mundo loquuntur et mundus eos audit. Nos ex Deo sumus. Qui novit Deum, audit nos; qui non est ex Deo, non audit nos. In hoc cognoscimus spiritum veritatis et spiritum erroris.
passages.english: Dearly beloved, believe not every spirit, but try the spirits if they be of God: because many false prophets are gone out into the world. By this is the spirit of God known. Every spirit that dissolveth Jesus, is not of God: and this is Antichrist, of whom you have heard that he cometh, and he is now already in the world. You are of God, little children, and have overcome him: because greater is he that is in you, than he that is in the world. They are of the world: therefore of the world they speak, and the world heareth them. We are of God. He that knoweth God, heareth us; he that is not of God, heareth us not. By this we know the spirit of truth and the spirit of error.
passages.notes: Discernment of spirits. Indirect question: probate spiritus si ex Deo sint (si + subjunctive = indirect question). Comparative: maior est qui in vobis... quam qui in mundo.

sentences: Probate spiritus si ex Deo sint.
Maior est qui in vobis est quam qui in mundo.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the indirect question. In sentence 2, identify the comparative construction.

parsing.answer: sum, esse — present active subjunctive, 3rd person plural; indirect question with si; 'whether they are'

parsing.prompt: Parse: maior (in 'maior est qui in vobis')
parsing.answer: magnus, -a, -um — comparative, masculine/feminine nominative singular (maior, maius); 'greater'
vinco, vincere — perfect active indicative, 2nd person plural, 3rd conjugation; 'you have overcome/conquered'
loquor, loqui — present indicative (deponent), 3rd person plural, 3rd conjugation; 'they speak'

identification.instruction: Find the comparative construction in 1 John 4:6. Identify the comparative adjective, its two terms of comparison (quam clause), and diagram the structure.
identification.answer: maior est [qui in vobis est] quam [qui in mundo (est)] — maior (comparative adj.); first term: qui in vobis est (he who is in you); quam (than); second term: qui in mundo (he who is in the world). Structure: [Subject] est maior quam [comparison].

passages.title: I Iōhannes 4:7–12 · Deus Cāritās Est
passages.latin: Carissimi, diligamus invicem, quia caritas ex Deo est, et omnis qui diligit ex Deo natus est et cognoscit Deum. Qui non diligit, non novit Deum, quoniam Deus caritas est. In hoc apparuit caritas Dei in nobis, quoniam Filium suum unigenitum misit Deus in mundum ut vivamus per eum. In hoc est caritas, non quasi nos dilexerimus Deum, sed quoniam ipse prior dilexit nos et misit Filium suum propitiationem pro peccatis nostris. Carissimi, si sic Deus dilexit nos, et nos debemus alterutrum diligere. Deum nemo vidit umquam. Si diligamus invicem, Deus in nobis manet et caritas eius in nobis perfecta est.
passages.english: Dearly beloved, let us love one another, for charity is of God. And every one that loveth, is born of God, and knoweth God. He that loveth not, knoweth not God: for God is charity. By this hath the charity of God appeared towards us, because God hath sent his only begotten Son into the world, that we may live by him. In this is charity: not as though we had loved God, but because he hath first loved us, and sent his Son to be a propitiation for our sins. My dearest, if God hath so loved us; we also ought to love one another. No man hath seen God at any time. If we love one another, God abideth in us, and his charity is perfected in us.
passages.notes: God is love — the theological center of 1 John. Gerundive implied: 'let us love' = hortatory subjunctive. Quasi + subjunctive: quasi nos dilexerimus (as if we had loved). Perfect passive: perfecta est.

sentences: Deus caritas est.
In hoc est caritas: quoniam ipse prior dilexit nos et misit Filium suum propitiationem pro peccatis nostris.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 2, identify: the indirect statement, the subject, and two main verbs.

parsing.answer: diligo, diligere — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural; hortatory subjunctive; 'let us love'
diligo, diligere — perfect active subjunctive, 1st person plural; 'as though we had loved' (past contrary-to-fact with quasi)
diligo, diligere — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular, 3rd conjugation; 'loved, has loved'
perficio, perficere — perfect passive indicative, 3rd person singular; 'has been perfected, is complete'

identification.instruction: Identify all uses of the hortatory subjunctive (first person plural subjunctive meaning 'let us...') in 1 John 4:7–12.
identification.answer: diligamus invicem (4:7 — 'let us love one another'); diligamus (4:11 — 'if we love' — conditional, not hortatory here). The hortatory subj. is diligamus invicem at the opening and echoed at the close.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 4:13–21 · In Cāritāte Manēmus
passages.latin: In hoc cognoscimus quoniam in eo manemus et ipse in nobis, quoniam de Spiritu suo dedit nobis. Et nos vidimus et testificamur quoniam Pater misit Filium Salvatorem mundi. Quisquis confessus fuerit quoniam Iesus est Filius Dei, Deus in eo manet et ipse in Deo. Et nos cognovimus et credidimus caritati quam habet Deus in nobis. Deus caritas est, et qui manet in caritate in Deo manet et Deus in eo manet. In hoc perfecta est caritas nobiscum, ut fiduciam habeamus in die iudicii. Timor non est in caritate, sed perfecta caritas foras mittit timorem, quoniam timor poenam habet; qui autem timet, non est perfectus in caritate. Nos ergo diligamus Deum, quoniam Deus prior nos dilexit. Si quis dixerit: Diligo Deum, et fratrem suum oderit, mendax est.
passages.english: In this we know that we abide in him, and he in us: because he hath given us of his spirit. And we have seen, and do testify, that the Father hath sent his Son to be the Saviour of the world. Whosoever shall confess that Jesus is the Son of God, God abideth in him, and he in God. And we have known, and have believed the charity, which God hath to us. God is charity: and he that abideth in charity, abideth in God, and God in him. In this is the charity of God perfected with us, that we may have confidence in the day of judgment. There is no fear in charity: but perfect charity casteth out fear, because fear hath pain. And he that feareth, is not perfected in charity. Let us therefore love God, because God first loved us. If any man say, I love God, and hateth his brother; he is a liar.
passages.notes: Perfect love casts out fear. Gerundive of obligation could be implied. Timor (fear) — 3rd decl. m. Quisquis confessus fuerit — future perfect + deponent. Caritas foras mittit — present active.

sentences: Perfecta caritas foras mittit timorem quoniam timor poenam habet.
Si quis dixerit: Diligo Deum, et fratrem suum oderit, mendax est.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify the deponent verb in sentence 1 passage context. In sentence 2, identify the condition type.

parsing.answer: confiteor, confiteri (deponent) — future perfect indicative (or perfect subjunctive), 3rd person singular; 'whoever shall have confessed'
odi, odisse — perfect active (used as present/future), 3rd person singular; defective verb; 'hates'
testificor, testificari (deponent) — present indicative, 1st person plural; 'we testify'
mitto, mittere — present active indicative, 3rd person singular, 3rd conjugation; 'casts out, sends away'

identification.instruction: Find all deponent verbs in 1 John 4:13–21. For each, give the infinitive, tense, and translation.
identification.answer: testificamur (testificor, testificari — pres. 'we testify'); confessus fuerit (confiteor, confiteri — fut. perf. 'shall have confessed'); note oderit is from defective odi, not deponent

passages.title: I Iōhannes 5:1–5 · Vīcit Mundum
passages.latin: Omnis qui credit quoniam Iesus est Christus ex Deo natus est; et omnis qui diligit eum qui genuit diligit et eum qui natus est ex eo. In hoc cognoscimus quoniam diligimus filios Dei, cum Deum diligamus et mandata eius faciamus. Haec est enim caritas Dei ut mandata eius custodiamus; et mandata eius gravia non sunt. Quoniam omne quod natum est ex Deo vincit mundum; et haec est victoria quae vincit mundum, fides nostra. Quis est qui vincit mundum, nisi qui credit quoniam Iesus est Filius Dei?
passages.english: Whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ, is born of God. And every one that loveth him who begot, loveth him also who is born of him. In this we know that we love the children of God: when we love God, and keep his commandments. For this is the charity of God, that we keep his commandments: and his commandments are not heavy. For whatsoever is born of God, overcometh the world: and this is the victory which overcometh the world, our faith. Who is he that overcometh the world, but he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God?
passages.notes: Faith conquers the world. Victoria — 1st decl. noun. Vincit — 3rd conjugation present active. Haec est victoria quae vincit — relative clause. Gravia — 3rd declension adjective (gravis, grave).

sentences: Haec est victoria quae vincit mundum, fides nostra.
Mandata eius gravia non sunt.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the relative clause. In sentence 2, identify the predicate adjective and its noun.

parsing.prompt: Parse: vincit (in 'haec est victoria quae vincit mundum')
parsing.answer: vinco, vincere — present active indicative, 3rd person singular, 3rd conjugation; 'overcomes, conquers'
custodio, custodire — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural, 4th conjugation; indirect command with ut; 'that we keep/guard'

parsing.prompt: Parse: gravia (in 'mandata eius gravia non sunt')
parsing.answer: gravis, grave — neuter nominative/accusative plural; 3rd declension adjective; predicate adjective agreeing with mandata

parsing.prompt: Parse: nata est / natum est (in 'quod natum est ex Deo')
parsing.answer: nascor, nasci (deponent) — perfect indicative, 3rd person singular neuter; 'that which was born'

identification.instruction: Find the relative clause in 1 John 5:1–5 that functions as a noun clause (substantive relative). Identify its antecedent.
identification.answer: haec est victoria quae vincit mundum — relative clause: quae vincit mundum; antecedent: victoria. Also: omne quod natum est ex Deo — quod natum est ex Deo is substantive relative ('whatever is born'), functioning as the subject of vincit.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 5:6–12 · Tēstimonium Trīnum
passages.latin: Hic est qui venit per aquam et sanguinem, Iesus Christus; non in aqua solum, sed in aqua et sanguine. Et Spiritus est qui testificatur, quoniam Spiritus est veritas. Quoniam tres sunt qui testimonium dant, Spiritus et aqua et sanguis, et hi tres unum sunt. Si testimonium hominum accipimus, testimonium Dei maius est; quoniam hoc est testimonium Dei, quod maius est, quoniam testificatus est de Filio suo. Qui credit in Filium Dei habet testimonium Dei in se. Qui non credit Filio Dei mendacem facit eum; quia non credidit in testimonium quod testificatus est Deus de Filio suo. Et hoc est testimonium: quoniam vitam aeternam dedit nobis Deus, et haec vita in Filio eius est. Qui habet Filium habet vitam; qui non habet Filium Dei vitam non habet.
passages.english: This is he that came by water and blood, Jesus Christ: not by water only, but by water and blood. And it is the Spirit which testifieth, that Christ is the truth. For there are Three who give testimony in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost. And these three are one. And there are three that give testimony on earth: the spirit, and the water, and the blood: and these three are one. If we receive the testimony of men, the testimony of God is greater. For this is the testimony of God, which is greater, because he hath testified of his Son. He that believeth in the Son of God, hath the testimony of God in himself. He that believeth not the Son of God, maketh him a liar: because he believeth not in the testimony which God hath testified of his Son. And this is the testimony, that God hath given to us eternal life: and this life is in his Son.
passages.notes: The three witnesses. Testimonium (witness, testimony) — 2nd decl. neuter, key vocabulary. Maius — comparative of magnus. Testificatus est — perfect deponent. Haec vita in Filio eius est.

sentences: Qui habet Filium habet vitam; qui non habet Filium Dei vitam non habet.
Hoc est testimonium: quoniam vitam aeternam dedit nobis Deus, et haec vita in Filio eius est.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the two parallel relative clauses. In sentence 2, identify the indirect statement.

parsing.answer: testificor, testificari (deponent) — perfect indicative, 3rd person singular; 'testified, has testified'
magnus, -a, -um — comparative neuter nominative singular (maior, maius); predicate adjective; 'greater'
mendax, mendacis — masculine/feminine accusative singular; predicate accusative ('makes him a liar')
credo, credere — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular, 3rd conjugation; 'believed, has believed'

identification.instruction: The verse 'Qui habet Filium habet vitam; qui non habet Filium Dei vitam non habet' is a perfect antithesis. Identify every grammatical parallel: subject, verb, object.
identification.answer: Parallel 1: [Qui habet Filium] [habet] [vitam] — relative subj., verb, obj. || Parallel 2: [qui non habet Filium Dei] [vitam non habet] — relative subj., negated verb, obj. inverted. Perfect chiasm: habet Filium / vitam non habet.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 5:13–17 · Ut Sciātis Vōs Habēre Vītam
passages.latin: Haec scribo vobis ut sciatis quoniam vitam aeternam habetis, qui creditis in nomine Filii Dei. Et haec est fiducia quam habemus ad eum, quia si quid petierimus secundum voluntatem eius, audit nos. Et scimus quia audit nos, quidquid petierimus; scimus quoniam habemus petitiones quas postulamus ab eo. Scit quis fratrem suum peccare peccato non ad mortem; petat, et dabitur ei vita pro his qui non peccant ad mortem. Est peccatum ad mortem; non pro illo dico ut roget quis. Omnis iniquitas peccatum est, et est peccatum ad mortem.
passages.english: These things I write to you, that you may know that you have eternal life, you who believe in the name of the Son of God. And this is the confidence which we have towards him: That, whatsoever we shall ask according to his will, he heareth us. And we know that he heareth us whatsoever we ask: we know that we have the petitions which we request of him. He that knoweth his brother to sin a sin which is not to death, let him ask, and life shall be given to him, who sinneth not to death. There is a sin unto death: for that I say not that any man ask. All iniquity is sin, and there is a sin unto death.
passages.notes: Confidence in prayer. Petitio (petition, request) — 3rd decl. f. Dabitur — future passive of do. Ut sciatis — purpose clause with perfect meaning. Indirect statement with infinitive: Scit fratrem suum peccare.

vocab_hints.english: will be given (fut. pass. of do)

sentences: Haec scribo vobis ut sciatis quoniam vitam aeternam habetis.
Si quid petierimus secundum voluntatem eius, audit nos.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify the purpose clause in sentence 1. Identify the condition type in sentence 2.

parsing.answer: scio, scire — present active subjunctive, 2nd person plural, 4th conjugation; purpose clause; 'that you may know'
do, dare — future passive indicative, 3rd person singular, 1st conjugation; 'will be given'
peto, petere — future perfect active indicative (or perfect subjunctive), 1st person plural, 3rd conjugation; conditional; 'if we shall have asked'
pecco, peccare — present active infinitive; 1st conjugation; indirect statement with scit ('knows his brother to sin')

identification.instruction: Review: Find one example each of (a) purpose clause, (b) indirect statement with infinitive, (c) future passive, and (d) future perfect in 1 John 5:13–17.
identification.answer: a) ut sciatis (purpose: 'that you may know'); b) Scit fratrem suum peccare (indirect statement: scit + acc. + inf.); c) dabitur (future passive: 'will be given'); d) petierimus (future perfect: 'shall have asked')

passages.title: I Iōhannes 5:18–21 · Custōdīte Vōs Ab Īdōlīs
passages.latin: Scimus quoniam omnis qui natus est ex Deo non peccat, sed generatio Dei conservat eum, et malignus non tangit eum. Scimus quoniam ex Deo sumus, et mundus totus in maligno positus est. Et scimus quoniam Filius Dei venit et dedit nobis sensum ut cognoscamus verum; et simus in vero, in Filio eius Iesu Christo. Hic est verus Deus et vita aeterna. Filioli, custodite vos ab idolis.
passages.english: We know that whosoever is born of God, sinneth not: but the generation of God preserveth him, and the wicked one toucheth him not. We know that we are of God, and the whole world is seated in wickedness. And we know that the Son of God is come, and hath given us understanding that we may know the true God, and may be in his true Son Jesus Christ. This is the true God and life eternal. Little children, keep yourselves from idols.
passages.notes: The closing of 1 John. Custodite (imperative plural). Simus — hortatory/purpose subjunctive. Sensum — 4th decl. accusative. Cognoscamus — purpose subjunctive. The letter ends with an abrupt warning: keep from idols.

sentences: Filius Dei venit et dedit nobis sensum ut cognoscamus verum.
Filioli, custodite vos ab idolis.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the purpose clause. In sentence 2, identify the imperative and its mood.

parsing.answer: cognosco, cognoscere — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural, 3rd conjugation; purpose clause with ut; 'that we may know'
sum, esse — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural; purpose/hortatory; 'and that we may be / let us be'
custodio, custodire — present active imperative, 2nd person plural, 4th conjugation; 'Keep! Guard!'
sensus, -us, m. — accusative singular; 4th declension; direct object of dedit ('gave us understanding')

identification.instruction: Scimus appears three times in 1 John 5:18–20. What does John claim to 'know' in each instance? What grammatical construction follows scimus each time?
identification.answer: 1. Scimus quoniam omnis qui natus est... non peccat (quoniam + indicative: indirect statement) — 'we know that everyone born of God does not sin'; 2. Scimus quoniam ex Deo sumus (quoniam + indicative) — 'we know that we are of God'; 3. Scimus quoniam Filius Dei venit (quoniam + indicative) — 'we know that the Son of God has come'. All three use quoniam + indicative for indirect statement.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 2:28–3:3 · Appārebit · Revisiō
passages.latin: Et nunc, filioli, manete in eo, ut cum apparuerit, habeamus fiduciam et non confundamur ab eo in adventu eius. Si scitis quoniam iustus est, scitote quoniam et omnis qui facit iustitiam ex ipso natus est. Videte qualem caritatem dedit nobis Pater, ut filii Dei nominemur et simus. Propter hoc mundus non novit nos, quia non novit eum. Carissimi, nunc filii Dei sumus, et nondum apparuit quid erimus. Scimus quoniam cum apparuerit, similes ei erimus, quoniam videbimus eum sicuti est. Et omnis qui habet spem hanc in eo, sanctificat se, sicut et ille sanctus est.
passages.english: And now, little children, abide in him, that when he shall appear, we may have confidence, and not be confounded by him at his coming. If you know that he is just, know ye, that every one also who doth justice, is born of him. Behold what manner of charity the Father hath bestowed upon us, that we should be called, and should be the sons of God. Therefore the world knoweth us not, because it knew him not. Dearly beloved, we are now the sons of God; and it hath not yet appeared what we shall be. We know, that, when he shall appear, we shall be like to him: because we shall see him as he is.
passages.notes: Review: purpose clauses, future tense, comparative adjectives. Cum apparuerit — temporal clause with future perfect. Similes ei — comparative adjective + dative. Ut ... habeamus, nominemur, simus — purpose clauses.

sentences: Scimus quoniam cum apparuerit similes ei erimus quoniam videbimus eum sicuti est.
Et omnis qui habet spem hanc in eo sanctificat se.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. Identify every purpose clause. Circle the comparative adjective and draw an arrow to the dative it governs.

parsing.answer: appareo, apparere — future perfect active indicative (or perfect subjunctive), 3rd person singular; temporal cum clause; 'when he shall have appeared'
similis, simile — masculine/feminine nominative plural; predicate adjective; 'we shall be like' (+ dative ei)
confundo, confundere — present passive subjunctive, 1st person plural; negative purpose with ut non; 'that we not be put to shame'
nomino, nominare — present passive subjunctive, 1st person plural; purpose clause; 'that we may be called'

identification.instruction: Review: List every subjunctive verb in 1 John 2:28–3:3. Identify each as purpose, indirect command, indirect question, or temporal.
identification.answer: habeamus (purpose: ut habeamus fiduciam); confundamur (negative purpose: ut non confundamur); nominemur (purpose: ut nominemur); simus (purpose: et simus filii Dei); apparuerit — actually fut. perf. indicative (temporal); erimus — future indicative (indirect statement after scimus)

passages.title: I Iōhannes 3:16–24 · Agāpē · Revisiō
passages.latin: In hoc cognovimus caritatem Dei, quoniam ille animam suam pro nobis posuit; et nos debemus pro fratribus animas ponere. Qui habuerit substantiam huius mundi et viderit fratrem suum necessitatem habere et clauserit viscera sua ab eo, quomodo caritas Dei manet in eo? Filioli mei, non diligamus verbo neque lingua, sed opere et veritate. In hoc cognoscimus quoniam ex veritate sumus, et in conspectu eius suadebimus corda nostra. Quoniam si reprehenderit nos cor nostrum, maior est Deus corde nostro et novit omnia. Carissimi, si cor nostrum non reprehenderit nos, fiduciam habemus ad Deum. Et hoc est mandatum eius, ut credamus in nomine Filii eius Iesu Christi, et diligamus alterutrum sicut dedit mandatum nobis.
passages.english: In this we have known the charity of God, because he hath laid down his life for us: and we ought to lay down our lives for the brethren. He that hath the substance of this world, and shall see his brother in need, and shall shut up his bowels from him: how doth the charity of God abide in him? My little children, let us not love in word, nor in tongue, but in deed, and in truth. In this we know that we are of the truth: and in his sight shall persuade our hearts. For if our heart reprehend us, God is greater than our heart, and knoweth all things. Dearly beloved, if our heart do not reprehend us, we have confidence towards God. And this is his commandment, that we should believe in the name of his Son Jesus Christ: and love one another.
passages.notes: Review: gerunds and gerundives; ablative absolute; indirect commands. Ponere animas — gerundive of obligation implied (debemus ponere). Opere et veritate — ablatives of manner.

sentences: Non diligamus verbo neque lingua sed opere et veritate.
Hoc est mandatum eius ut credamus in nomine Filii eius Iesu Christi et diligamus alterutrum.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the ablatives of manner. In sentence 2, identify the indirect commands (ut + subj.).

parsing.answer: diligo, diligere — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural; hortatory subjunctive; 'let us love'
pono, ponere — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular, 3rd conjugation; 'laid down, placed'
credo, credere — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural; indirect command with ut; 'that we believe'
reprehendo, reprehendere — future perfect active (conditional si clause), 3rd person singular; 'if [our heart] shall have reproached'

identification.instruction: Review: Find examples of (a) hortatory subjunctive, (b) ablative of manner, (c) indirect command, and (d) comparative adjective in 1 John 3:16–24.
identification.answer: a) Hortatory: non diligamus (let us not love); b) Ablative of manner: opere et veritate ('in deed and truth' — no preposition, ablative alone); c) Indirect command: ut credamus... et diligamus (indirect command after mandatum); d) Comparative: maior est Deus corde nostro ('God is greater than our heart' — maior + ablative of comparison)

passages.title: I Iōhannes 4:7–5:5 · Deus Cāritās Est · Revisiō Finālis
passages.latin: Carissimi, diligamus invicem, quia caritas ex Deo est. Qui non diligit, non novit Deum, quoniam Deus caritas est. In hoc apparuit caritas Dei in nobis, quoniam Filium suum unigenitum misit Deus in mundum ut vivamus per eum. Carissimi, si sic Deus dilexit nos, et nos debemus alterutrum diligere. Deum nemo vidit umquam. Si diligamus invicem, Deus in nobis manet et caritas eius in nobis perfecta est. In hoc cognoscimus quoniam in eo manemus et ipse in nobis, quoniam de Spiritu suo dedit nobis. Timor non est in caritate, sed perfecta caritas foras mittit timorem. Nos ergo diligamus Deum, quoniam Deus prior nos dilexit. Omnis qui credit quoniam Iesus est Christus ex Deo natus est. Haec est enim caritas Dei ut mandata eius custodiamus. Quoniam omne quod natum est ex Deo vincit mundum; et haec est victoria quae vincit mundum, fides nostra.
passages.english: Dearly beloved, let us love one another, for charity is of God. He that loveth not, knoweth not God: for God is charity. By this hath the charity of God appeared towards us, because God hath sent his only begotten Son into the world, that we may live by him. My dearest, if God hath so loved us; we also ought to love one another. No man hath seen God at any time. If we love one another, God abideth in us, and his charity is perfected in us. In this we know that we abide in him, and he in us: because he hath given us of his spirit. There is no fear in charity: but perfect charity casteth out fear. Let us therefore love God, because God first loved us. Whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ, is born of God. For this is the charity of God, that we keep his commandments. For whatsoever is born of God, overcometh the world: and this is the victory which overcometh the world, our faith.
passages.notes: Final review of the entire 1 John course arc. This passage draws together the letter's key themes and all major grammatical constructions covered in Second Form: hortatory subjunctive, purpose clauses, indirect statement, comparatives, deponents, conditionals, perfect passive, future tense.

sentences: Deus caritas est, et qui manet in caritate in Deo manet et Deus in eo manet.
Haec est victoria quae vincit mundum, fides nostra.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. For sentence 1: identify every relative clause and its antecedent. For sentence 2: identify the relative clause used as an appositive.

parsing.answer: diligo, diligere — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural; hortatory ('let us love'); 3rd conjugation

parsing.prompt: Parse: perfecta est (in 'caritas eius in nobis perfecta est')
parsing.answer: perficio, perficere — perfect passive indicative, 3rd person singular; 'has been perfected, is complete'
custodio, custodire — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural, 4th conjugation; indirect command with ut; 'that we keep'
vinco, vincere — present active indicative, 3rd person singular, 3rd conjugation; 'overcomes, conquers'

identification.instruction: Final review: In this passage, find one example of each construction: (a) hortatory subjunctive, (b) purpose clause, (c) indirect statement, (d) perfect passive, (e) comparative, (f) deponent verb.
identification.answer: a) Hortatory: diligamus invicem ('let us love one another'); b) Purpose: ut vivamus per eum ('that we might live through him'); c) Indirect statement: scimus quoniam in eo manemus (quoniam + indicative); d) Perfect passive: perfecta est ('has been perfected'); e) Comparative: prior dilexit ('first loved' — prior is comparative of prae); f) Deponent: natus est (nascor — 'was born')

passages.title: II Iōhannes 1:1–6 · Dīligāmus Alter Alterum
passages.latin: Senior electae dominae et natis eius, quos ego diligo in veritate, et non ego solus, sed et omnes qui cognoverunt veritatem; propter veritatem quae permanet in nobis et nobiscum erit in aeternum. Sit nobiscum gratia, misericordia, pax a Deo Patre et a Iesu Christo Filio Patris in veritate et caritate. Gavisus sum valde quod inveni de filiis tuis ambulantes in veritate, sicut mandatum accepimus a Patre. Et nunc rogo te, domina, non tamquam mandatum novum scribens tibi, sed quod habuimus ab initio, ut diligamus alterutrum. Et haec est caritas ut ambulemus secundum mandata eius.
passages.english: The ancient to the lady Elect, and her children, whom I love in the truth, and not I only, but also all they that have known the truth: For the sake of the truth which dwelleth in us, and shall be with us for ever. Grace be with you, mercy, and peace from God the Father, and from Jesus Christ the Son of the Father, in truth and in charity. I was exceeding glad, that I found of thy children walking in the truth, as we have received a commandment from the Father. And now I beseech thee, lady, not as writing a new commandment to thee, but that which we have had from the beginning, that we love one another. And this is charity, that we walk according to his commandments.
passages.notes: Second Epistle of John. Indirect statement: quod inveni de filiis tuis ambulantes (acc. + participle construction). Gavisus sum — perfect deponent (gaudeo). Rogo te ut diligamus — indirect command.

sentences: Gavisus sum valde quod inveni de filiis tuis ambulantes in veritate.
Et haec est caritas ut ambulemus secundum mandata eius.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the participle and its case. In sentence 2, identify the indirect command.

parsing.answer: gaudeo, gaudere (semi-deponent) — perfect indicative, 1st person singular; 'I rejoiced, was glad'
ambulo, ambulare — present active participle, accusative plural; 1st conjugation; 'walking' (indirect statement with inveni)
diligo, diligere — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural; indirect command with ut; 'that we love'
permaneo, permanere — present active indicative, 3rd person singular, 2nd conjugation; 'abides, remains'

identification.instruction: Find the accusative + participle construction in 2 John 1–6 (indirect statement). Identify the governing verb, the accusative noun, and the participle.
identification.answer: quod inveni de filiis tuis ambulantes in veritate — governing verb: inveni ('I found'); accusative: filiis tuis (implied subject of participle); participle: ambulantes (acc. pl. — 'walking'). Indirect statement: 'I found your children walking in truth.'

passages.title: II Iōhannes 1:7–13 · Antīchristī in Mundum Exiērunt
passages.latin: Quoniam multi seductores exierunt in mundum, qui non confitentur Iesum Christum venientem in carnem; hic est seductor et antichristus. Videte vosmetipsos ne perdatis quae operati estis, sed ut mercedem plenam accipiatis. Omnis qui recedit et non manet in doctrina Christi, Deum non habet. Qui manet in doctrina, hic et Patrem et Filium habet. Si quis venit ad vos et hanc doctrinam non affert, nolite recipere eum in domum, et ave ei ne dixeritis. Qui enim dicit illi ave, communicat operibus eius malignis. Plura habens vobis scribere, nolui per chartam et atramentum; spero enim me ad vos venturum et os ad os loqui.
passages.english: For many seducers are gone out into the world, who confess not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh: this is a seducer and an antichrist. Look to yourselves, that you lose not the things which you have wrought: but that you may receive a full reward. Whosoever revolteth, and continueth not in the doctrine of Christ, hath not God. He that continueth in the doctrine, the same hath both the Father and the Son. If any man come to you, and bring not this doctrine, receive him not into the house nor say to him, God speed you. For he that saith unto him, God speed you, communicateth with his wicked works.
passages.notes: Warning against false teachers. Gerundive: quae operati estis (things you have worked). Future infinitive: me venturum — indirect statement with spero. Nolite + infinitive — negative command.

sentences: Qui manet in doctrina hic et Patrem et Filium habet.
Spero enim me ad vos venturum et os ad os loqui.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 2, identify the future infinitive (venturum) and the indirect statement. What verb governs the infinitive?

parsing.answer: venio, venire — future active infinitive (actually future active participle in accusative: venturum), 4th conjugation; indirect statement with spero; 'that I am about to come'
loquor, loqui (deponent) — present passive infinitive (deponent); 3rd conjugation; 'to speak'
dico, dicere — perfect active subjunctive (or future perfect), 2nd person plural; prohibition with ne; 'do not say'
recipio, recipere — present active infinitive; 3rd conjugation -io; complementary infinitive with nolite

identification.instruction: Find the indirect statement in 2 John 7–13 that uses a future infinitive. Identify the governing verb, the accusative subject, and the future infinitive.
identification.answer: spero enim me ad vos venturum [esse] — governing verb: spero; acc. subject: me; future infinitive: venturum (esse) — 'I hope that I am about to come to you.'

passages.title: III Iōhannes 1:1–8 · Ambulāre in Vēritāte
passages.latin: Senior Gaio carissimo, quem ego diligo in veritate. Carissime, de omnibus orationem facio ut prosperetur et valeas, sicut prosperatur anima tua. Gavisus sum valde venientibus fratribus et testimonium perhibentibus veritati tuae, sicut tu in veritate ambulas. Maiorem horum non habeo gratiam, quam ut audiam filios meos in veritate ambulare. Carissime, fideliter facis quidquid operaris in fratres, et hoc in peregrinos, qui testimonium reddiderunt caritati tuae in conspectu Ecclesiae. Quos si deduces in itinere digne Deo, bene facies. Pro nomine enim eius profecti sunt nihil accipientes a Gentibus. Nos ergo suscipere debemus huiusmodi, ut cooperatores simus veritatis.
passages.english: The ancient to the dearest Gaius, whom I love in truth. Dearly beloved, I pray that thou mayest advance in all things and fare well, as thy soul doth advance. I was exceeding glad when the brethren came and gave testimony to the truth in thee, even as thou walkest in the truth. I have no greater grace than this, to hear that my children walk in truth. Dearly beloved, thou dost faithfully whatever thou dost for the brethren, and for strangers, who have given testimony of thy charity in the sight of the church: whom if thou bring forward on their way worthy of God, thou shalt do well. Because for his name they went out, taking nothing of the Gentiles. We therefore ought to receive such, that we may be fellow-helpers of the truth.
passages.notes: Third Epistle of John. Gerund: audiam filios meos ambulare (indirect statement). Suscipere debemus — debeo + infinitive. Ut cooperatores simus — purpose clause.

sentences: Maiorem horum non habeo gratiam quam ut audiam filios meos in veritate ambulare.
Nos ergo suscipere debemus huiusmodi ut cooperatores simus veritatis.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the indirect statement (audiam + inf.). In sentence 2, identify debeo + infinitive and the purpose clause.

parsing.prompt: Parse: profecti sunt (in 'pro nomine profecti sunt')
parsing.answer: proficiscor, proficisci (deponent) — perfect indicative, 3rd person plural; 'they set out, departed'
sum, esse — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural; purpose clause with ut; 'that we may be'
ambulo, ambulare — present active infinitive; 1st conjugation; indirect statement with audiam ('that my children walk')
debeo, debere — present active indicative, 1st person plural, 2nd conjugation; 'we ought, owe'

identification.instruction: Find all deponent verbs in 3 John 1–8. For each, give the first person singular, infinitive, and perfect form.
identification.answer: proficiscor, proficisci, profectus sum ('to set out' — profecti sunt in text). Note: gavisus sum (gaudeo — semi-deponent, pf. 'rejoiced'). loqui (loquor) would appear if present.

passages.title: III Iōhannes 1:9–15 · Imitāre Bonum
passages.latin: Scripsi forsitan Ecclesiae; sed is qui amat primatum gerere in eis, Diotrephes, non recipit nos. Propter hoc si venero, commonebo eius opera quae facit, verbis malignis garriens in nos; et quasi non sufficiat ei haec, neque ipse suscipit fratres, et eos qui suscipiunt prohibet, et de Ecclesia eicit. Carissime, noli imitari malum, sed quod bonum est. Qui bene facit, ex Deo est; qui male facit, non vidit Deum. Demetrio testimonium redditum est ab omnibus et ab ipsa veritate; sed et nos testimonium perhibemus, et scis quoniam testimonium nostrum verum est. Habebam multa tibi scribere, sed nolo per atramentum et calamum scribere tibi. Spero autem protinus te videre et os ad os loquemur. Pax tibi. Salutant te amici. Saluta amicos nominatim.
passages.english: I had written perhaps to the church: but Diotrephes, who loveth to have the pre-eminence among them, doth not receive us. For this cause, if I come, I will advertise his works which he doth, with malicious words prating against us; and as if these were not enough for him, neither doth he himself receive the brethren, and them that do receive them he forbiddeth, and casteth out of the church. Dearly beloved, follow not that which is evil, but that which is good. He that doth good, is of God: he that doth evil, hath not seen God. To Demetrius testimony was given by all men, and by the truth itself; yea, and we also give testimony; and thou knowest that our testimony is true. I had many things to write unto thee, but I will not by ink and pen write to thee. But I hope to see thee shortly, and we shall speak face to face. Peace be to thee. The friends salute thee. Salute the friends by name.
passages.notes: Concluding exhortation. Noli + infinitive — negative command (singular). Gerundive: multa tibi scribere — gerundive of purpose. Testimonium redditum est — perfect passive. Loquemur — future deponent.

vocab_hints.english: to not want; noli + inf. = do not! (sg.)
has been given (perf. pass. of reddo)

sentences: Carissime, noli imitari malum sed quod bonum est.
Testimonium redditum est ab omnibus et ab ipsa veritate.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the negative command form (noli + inf.). In sentence 2, identify the perfect passive and the agent (ab + ablative).

parsing.answer: nolo, nolle — present active imperative, 2nd person singular; 'Do not!' (negative command with infinitive)
imitor, imitari (deponent) — present infinitive (passive form, active meaning); 'to imitate'
reddo, reddere — perfect passive indicative, 3rd person singular; 'was given, has been rendered'
loquor, loqui (deponent) — future indicative, 1st person plural; 'we shall speak'

identification.instruction: Find both forms of negative command in 3 John 1–15 (noli and nolite). State the difference between them and translate each.
identification.answer: noli imitari malum (3 Jn 1:11) — noli + inf., 2nd sg. singular command: 'Do not imitate evil' (addressed to Gaius, one person); nolite is plural (not present here but in 1 John: nolite diligere mundum). Noli = singular; nolite = plural.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 2:1–6 · Māndata Eius Observāmus · Participiīs
passages.latin: Filioli mei, haec scribo vobis ut non peccetis. Sed et si quis peccaverit, advocatum habemus apud Patrem Iesum Christum iustum; et ipse est propitiatio pro peccatis nostris. Et in hoc scimus quoniam cognovimus eum, si mandata eius observamus. Qui dicit quoniam novit eum, et mandata eius non observat, mendax est, et in hoc veritas non est. Qui autem servat verbum eius, vere in hoc caritas Dei perfecta est. In hoc scimus quoniam in ipso sumus. Qui dicit se in ipso manere, debet, sicut ille ambulavit, et ipse ambulare.
passages.english: My little children, these things I write to you, that you may not sin. But if any man sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the just. And he is the propitiation for our sins: and not for ours only, but also for those of the whole world. And by this we know that we have known him, if we keep his commandments. He who saith that he knoweth him, and keepeth not his commandments, is a liar, and the truth is not in him. But he that keepeth his word, in him the charity of God is truly perfected: and by this we know that we are in him. He that saith he abideth in him, ought himself also to walk, even as he walked.
passages.notes: Review with focus on participles. Qui dicit, qui servat, qui manet — all present participles functioning as substantives ('the one who...'). Perfect participle: cognovimus.

sentences: Qui dicit se in ipso manere debet sicut ille ambulavit et ipse ambulare.
Qui autem servat verbum eius vere in hoc caritas Dei perfecta est.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the indirect statement (se manere). Circle every relative clause (qui + verb). Underline the substantive use of qui ('he who').

parsing.answer: pecco, peccare — present active subjunctive, 2nd person plural; purpose clause; 'that you may not sin'
maneo, manere — present active infinitive; indirect statement with dicit; 'to remain, abide'
ambulo, ambulare — perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular, 1st conjugation; 'walked'
perficio, perficere — perfect passive indicative, 3rd person singular; 'has been perfected'

identification.instruction: Find all substantive relative clauses in 1 John 2:1–6 — clauses beginning with qui/quae/quod where the antecedent is the whole clause ('whoever', 'he who'). List each.
identification.answer: Qui dicit quoniam novit eum et mandata non observat (= 'the one who says...'); Qui autem servat verbum eius (= 'whoever keeps his word'); Qui dicit se in ipso manere (= 'whoever says he abides in him'). All three are substantive relatives with no explicit antecedent.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 4:1–6 · Māior Est Quī in Vōbīs · Comparātiō
passages.latin: Carissimi, nolite omni spiritui credere, sed probate spiritus si ex Deo sint, quoniam multi pseudoprophetae exierunt in mundum. In hoc cognoscitur Spiritus Dei: omnis spiritus qui confitetur Iesum Christum in carne venisse, ex Deo est. Et omnis spiritus qui solvit Iesum ex Deo non est, et hic est antichristus quem audistis quia venit, et nunc iam in mundo est. Vos ex Deo estis, filioli, et vicistis eum, quoniam maior est qui in vobis est quam qui in mundo. Ipsi de mundo sunt; ideo de mundo loquuntur et mundus eos audit. Nos ex Deo sumus. Qui novit Deum, audit nos; qui non est ex Deo, non audit nos. In hoc cognoscimus spiritum veritatis et spiritum erroris.
passages.english: Dearly beloved, believe not every spirit, but try the spirits if they be of God: because many false prophets are gone out into the world. By this is the spirit of God known. Every spirit that dissolveth Jesus, is not of God. You are of God, little children, and have overcome him: because greater is he that is in you, than he that is in the world. They are of the world: therefore of the world they speak. We are of God. He that knoweth God, heareth us. In this we know the spirit of truth and the spirit of error.
passages.notes: Second pass with focus on comparatives and superlatives. Maior est... quam — comparative + quam. Spiritus veritatis vs. spiritus erroris — genitive of description.

sentences: Maior est qui in vobis est quam qui in mundo.
In hoc cognoscimus spiritum veritatis et spiritum erroris.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, diagram the comparison: identify the comparative adjective, the two terms being compared, and the quam. In sentence 2, identify both genitives of description.

parsing.answer: magnus, -a, -um — comparative, masculine/feminine nominative singular (maior, maius); predicate adjective; 'greater'
sum, esse — present active subjunctive, 3rd person plural; indirect question with si; 'whether they are'
venio, venire — perfect active infinitive; indirect statement with confitetur; 'to have come'
loquor, loqui (deponent) — present indicative, 3rd person plural; 'they speak'

identification.instruction: Identify three different types of genitive in 1 John 4:1–6: (a) genitive of description, (b) genitive of possession, and (c) subjective/objective genitive.
identification.answer: a) Genitive of description: spiritum veritatis ('spirit of truth' — defines character); spiritum erroris ('spirit of error'); b) Genitive of possession: Spiritus Dei ('Spirit of God — belonging to God'); c) Could argue Dei in confitetur Iesum Christum... ex Deo as source/origin rather than possession

passages.title: I Iōhannes 3:1–8 · Ablātīvus Absolūtus
passages.latin: Videte qualem caritatem dedit nobis Pater, ut filii Dei nominemur et simus. Propter hoc mundus non novit nos, quia non novit eum. Carissimi, nunc filii Dei sumus, et nondum apparuit quid erimus. Scimus quoniam cum apparuerit, similes ei erimus, quoniam videbimus eum sicuti est. Et omnis qui habet spem hanc in eo, sanctificat se, sicut et ille sanctus est. Filioli, nemo vos seducat. Qui facit iustitiam iustus est, sicut et ille iustus est. Qui facit peccatum ex diabolo est, quoniam ab initio diabolus peccat. In hoc apparuit Filius Dei, ut dissolvat opera diaboli.
passages.english: Behold what manner of charity the Father hath bestowed upon us, that we should be called, and should be the sons of God. Therefore the world knoweth us not, because it knew him not. Dearly beloved, we are now the sons of God; and it hath not yet appeared what we shall be. We know, that, when he shall appear, we shall be like to him: because we shall see him as he is. And every one that hath this hope in him, sanctifieth himself, as he also is holy. Little children, let no man deceive you. He that doth justice is just, even as he is just. He that committeth sin is of the devil: for the devil sinneth from the beginning.
passages.notes: Ablative absolute focus. Although no true ablative absolute appears, use the passage to practice creating and recognizing them. Also: cum + subjunctive temporal clause.

sentences: Cum apparuerit similes ei erimus quoniam videbimus eum sicuti est.
In hoc apparuit Filius Dei ut dissolvat opera diaboli.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the temporal cum clause and the future tense verbs. In sentence 2, identify the purpose clause.

parsing.answer: appareo, apparere — future perfect active indicative, 3rd person singular; temporal cum clause; 'when he shall have appeared'
sum, esse — future active indicative, 1st person plural; 'we shall be'
seduco, seducere — present active subjunctive, 3rd person singular; jussive subjunctive; 'let no one deceive you'
dissolvo, dissolvere — present active subjunctive, 3rd person singular; purpose clause with ut; 'that he might destroy'

identification.instruction: Practice: Take the sentence 'Filio apparente, filii Dei fuimus.' Identify the ablative absolute and translate it. Then explain why no true ablative absolute appears in 1 John 3:1–8 (hint: look at the cum clause).
identification.answer: Practice sentence: Filio apparente = ablative absolute ('the Son appearing' — abl. noun + abl. participle); translation: 'The Son having appeared, we were children of God.' — In the actual text, John uses cum apparuerit (temporal cum + subjunctive) instead of an ablative absolute, which is more common in classical and Vulgate Latin for temporal relationships.

passages.title: I Iōhannes 5:1–12 · Victōria Fideī · Gerundīva
passages.latin: Omnis qui credit quoniam Iesus est Christus ex Deo natus est. In hoc cognoscimus quoniam diligimus filios Dei, cum Deum diligamus et mandata eius faciamus. Haec est enim caritas Dei ut mandata eius custodiamus; et mandata eius gravia non sunt. Quoniam omne quod natum est ex Deo vincit mundum; et haec est victoria quae vincit mundum, fides nostra. Quis est qui vincit mundum, nisi qui credit quoniam Iesus est Filius Dei? Et Spiritus est qui testificatur, quoniam Spiritus est veritas. Qui credit in Filium Dei habet testimonium Dei in se. Et hoc est testimonium: quoniam vitam aeternam dedit nobis Deus, et haec vita in Filio eius est. Qui habet Filium habet vitam; qui non habet Filium Dei vitam non habet.
passages.english: Whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ, is born of God. In this we know that we love the children of God: when we love God, and keep his commandments. For this is the charity of God, that we keep his commandments: and his commandments are not heavy. For whatsoever is born of God, overcometh the world: and this is the victory which overcometh the world, our faith. Who is he that overcometh the world, but he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God? And it is the Spirit which testifieth, that Christ is the truth. He that believeth in the Son of God, hath the testimony of God in himself. And this is the testimony, that God hath given to us eternal life: and this life is in his Son. He that hath the Son hath life; he that hath not the Son of God hath not life.
passages.notes: Review with gerund/gerundive focus. Mandata custodienda sunt — gerundive of obligation (implied). Haec est caritas... ut custodiamus — indirect command. Victoria quae vincit — substantive relative.

sentences: Haec est victoria quae vincit mundum, fides nostra.
Qui habet Filium habet vitam; qui non habet Filium Dei vitam non habet.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence. In sentence 1, identify the appositional relationship (fides nostra = haec victoria). In sentence 2, identify the chiasm (inverted parallel structure).

parsing.answer: testificor, testificari (deponent) — present indicative, 3rd person singular; 'testifies'
custodio, custodire — present active subjunctive, 1st person plural, 4th conjugation; indirect command with ut; 'that we keep'
vinco, vincere — present active indicative, 3rd person singular, 3rd conjugation; 'overcomes'

parsing.prompt: Parse: natum est (in 'quod natum est ex Deo')
parsing.answer: nascor, nasci (deponent) — perfect indicative, 3rd person singular, neuter; 'that which was born'

identification.instruction: Identify the gerundive of obligation (passive periphrastic) that is implied but not stated in 1 John 5:1–12. What would it look like if John had written it explicitly?
identification.answer: Implied: mandata eius custodienda sunt — 'His commandments must be kept' (gerundive custodienda + sunt). John instead writes haec est caritas Dei ut custodiamus — expressing the same idea as an indirect command rather than a gerundive. The gerundive would be: mandata custodienda sunt nobis ('the commandments are to be kept by us').

passages.title: I Iōhannes 1:1–5:21 · Omnia Revisiō · Iōhannēs Scrīpsit
passages.latin: Quod erat ab initio, quod audivimus, quod vidimus, annuntiamus vobis vitam aeternam. Deus lux est. Si in luce ambulamus societatem habemus et sanguis Iesu Filii eius mundat nos ab omni peccato. Si confitemur peccata nostra, fidelis est et iustus ut remittat nobis peccata. Advocatum habemus apud Patrem Iesum Christum iustum. Mandatum novum non scribo: ut diligamus alterutrum. Nolite diligere mundum. Nunc filii Dei sumus. Qui facit peccatum ex diabolo est; in hoc apparuit Filius Dei ut dissolvat opera diaboli. Non diligamus verbo sed opere et veritate. Deus caritas est. Perfecta caritas foras mittit timorem. Nos diligamus Deum quoniam ipse prior nos dilexit. Haec est victoria quae vincit mundum, fides nostra. Haec scribo vobis ut sciatis quoniam vitam aeternam habetis. Filioli, custodite vos ab idolis.
passages.english: That which was from the beginning, which we have heard, which we have seen, we declare to you eternal life. God is light. If we walk in the light we have fellowship and the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin. If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us. We have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the just. I write not a new commandment: that we love one another. Love not the world. We are now children of God. Whoever commits sin is of the devil; for this purpose the Son of God appeared, that he might destroy the works of the devil. Let us not love in word but in deed and truth. God is love. Perfect love casts out fear. Let us love God because he first loved us. This is the victory that overcomes the world, our faith. I write these things that you may know you have eternal life. Little children, keep yourselves from idols.
passages.source_story: Vulgate, 1 John (selected verses from all 5 chapters — final review)
passages.notes: Final lesson: complete review of 1 John. A mosaic of the letter's key verses, covering every grammatical construction from Second Form Latin. Suitable for oral review and translation practice.

sentences: Haec est victoria quae vincit mundum, fides nostra.
Filioli, custodite vos ab idolis.
Deus caritas est.
copywork.instructions: Copy each sentence from memory if possible. These are the key verses of 1 John. Identify every grammatical construction you recognize.

parsing.answer: mundo, mundare — present active indicative, 3rd person singular, 1st conjugation; 'cleanses'
dissolvo, dissolvere — present active subjunctive, 3rd person singular; purpose clause; 'that he might destroy'
custodio, custodire — present active imperative, 2nd person plural, 4th conjugation; 'Keep! Guard yourselves!'
scio, scire — present active subjunctive, 2nd person plural, 4th conjugation; purpose clause; 'that you may know'

identification.instruction: Final mastery review: In this collected passage, find one example of each construction learned in Second Form Latin: (a) hortatory subj., (b) purpose clause, (c) indirect statement, (d) negative command, (e) perfect passive, (f) deponent verb, (g) comparative adjective.
identification.answer: a) diligamus Deum ('let us love God' — hortatory); b) ut dissolvat opera diaboli (purpose); c) annuntiamus vobis vitam... / ut sciatis quoniam vitam habetis (indirect statement); d) Nolite diligere mundum (nolite + inf. — neg. command); e) mundat is active — use 'si confitemur... ut remittat' context; perfect passive: annuntiamus (quod vidimus implies perf.); f) confitemur (confiteor — deponent); g) prior dilexit ('first loved' — prior is comparative of prae)
